blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلصَّـٰٓفَّـٰتِ صَفًّا (١)
By the [angels] ranged in ranks, (1)
فَٱلزَّٰجِرَٰتِ زَجْرًا (٢)
by the ones who drive [the clouds] vigorously, (2)
فَٱلتَّـٰلِيَـٰتِ ذِكْرًا (٣)
by the ones who recite the reminder: (3)
إِنَّ إِلَـٰهَكُمْ لَوَٰحِدٌ (٤)
indeed your God is certainly One, (4)
رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَرَبُّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ (٥)
the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and the Lord of the easts. (5)
إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِزِينَةٍ ٱلْكَوَاكِبِ (٦)
Indeed We have adorned the lowest heaven with the finery of the stars, (6)
وَحِفْظًا مِّن كُلِّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّارِدٍ (٧)
and to guard from any froward devil. (7)
لَّا يَسَّمَّعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ وَيُقْذَفُونَ مِن كُلِّ جَانِبٍ (٨)
They do not eavesdrop on the Supernal Elite—they are shot at from every side, (8)
دُحُورًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ وَاصِبٌ (٩)
to drive them away, and there is a perpetual punishment for them— (9)
إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ ٱلْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُۥ شِهَابٌ ثَاقِبٌ (١٠)
except any who snatches a snatch, whereat a piercing flame pursues him. (10)
فَٱسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَهُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَم مَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ ۚ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّن طِينٍ لَّازِبٍۭ (١١)
Ask them, is their creation more prodigious or [that of other creatures] that We have created? Indeed, We created them from a viscous clay. (11)

(Commentary)

This surah contains an argumentation for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — a warning to the polytheists, and glad tidings for the sincere among the believers. It clarifies the ultimate fate of both groups. It then mentions several of His faithful servants upon whom Allah bestowed His favor and decreed that He would grant them victory over their enemies. The conclusion of the surah serves as a summary of its purpose: hallowing Him, invoking peace upon His messenger-servants, and praising Him, the Exalted, for what He has done. The surah is Meccan, as evidenced by its stylistic context (السياق).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالصَّافَّاتِ صَفًّا فَالزَّاجِراتِ زَجْراً فَالتَّالِياتِ ذِكْراً — "By those [angels] set in ranks, by those who drive [the clouds] away, by those who recite the Reminder" (37:1–3). The term الصافات (those set in ranks) is said to be the plural of saffah (صافة), which is the plural of saff (صاف). In any case, it refers to a group whose members arrange themselves in ranks. The term الزاجرات (those who drive away) is derived from zajr (الزجر), which means to divert someone from something by instilling fear through censure or punishment. The term التاليات (those who recite) is derived from tilawah (التلاوة), meaning recitation.

Allah, the Exalted, has sworn by these three groups: the Saffat, the Zajirat, and the Taliyat. Opinions differ regarding what they refer to. As for the Saffat, it is said they are the angels who arrange themselves in ranks in the heavens, similar to the ranks of the believers in prayer. It is also said they are the angels who spread their wings in the air when they intend to descend to the earth, standing in wait for the command of Allah, the Exalted. Others say they are the groups of believers who stand in ranks during prayer or in jihad.

As for the Zajirat, it is said they are the angels who deter servants from acts of disobedience; Allah conveys this to the hearts of people in the form of passing thoughts (الخطرات), just as He conveys the whisperings of the devils. It is also said they are the angels entrusted with the clouds, driving and steering them to wherever Allah, the Glorified, wills. Others say they are the deterrents of the Quran, namely its verses that forbid foul deeds. It is also said they are the believers who raise their voices with the Quran during its recitation, thereby deterring people from forbidden acts.

As for the Taliyat, it is said they are the angels who recite the revelation (الوحي) to the prophet to whom it is revealed. It is also said they are the angels who recite the Book written by Allah, which contains the mention of events. Others say they are the groups of Quran-reciters who recite it during prayer.

It is possible — and Allah knows best — that these three groups mentioned in the verses refer to the groups of angels who descend with the revelation, who are commanded to secure the path and repel the devils from interfering with it, and to deliver it to the prophets in general, or specifically to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَحاطَ بما لَدَيْهِمْ — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and another behind him, so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord, and He encompasses all that is with them" (72:26–28).

Accordingly, the meaning would be: I swear by the angels who arrange themselves in ranks along the path of revelation, and by those who repel the devils and prevent them from interfering in the revelation, and by those who recite the Reminder (الذكر) to the Prophet — which is either revelation in general or the Quran specifically, as supported by the expression "reciting the Reminder."

What we have mentioned is further supported by the mention of the devils being pelted with flaming fires (الشهب) immediately after these verses, as well as His subsequent saying: فَاسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَهُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقاً أَمْ مَنْ خَلَقْنا — "So ask them: Is their creation more prodigious, or [that of] others We have created?" (37:11), as we shall point out.

This does not contradict the attribution of the Quran's descent to Gabriel alone in His saying: مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — "Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart" (2:97), and His saying: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — "The Trustworthy Spirit brought it down to your heart" (26:193–194). This is because the aforementioned angels are the assistants of Gabriel; thus, their descent with it is his descent with it. Allah, the Exalted, has said: فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ — "In honored scriptures, exalted and purified, by the hands of scribes, noble and pious" (80:13–16). He also said, narrating their words: وَما نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ — "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord" (19:64), and He said: وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ — "Indeed we are those who stand in ranks, and indeed we are those who celebrate [His] glory" (37:165–166). This is similar to attributing the taking of souls to the messengers among the angels in His saying: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا — "Until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him" (6:61), while also attributing it to the Angel of Death, who is their chief, in His saying: قُلْ يَتَوفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ — "Say, 'The Angel of Death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls'" (32:11).

There is no harm in referring to the angels with feminine forms — al-Saffat, al-Zajirat, and al-Taliyat — because the described subject is the "group" (الجماعة), and the femininity is merely grammatical (لفظي).

This is the first surah in the Quran to begin with an oath. Allah, the Glorified, has sworn in His speech by many of His creations, such as the heaven, the earth, the sun, the moon, the stars, the night, the day, the angels, humans, cities, and fruits. This is only due to the nobility (الشرف) they possess by virtue of their creation being attributed to Him, the Exalted, for He is their Sustainer (القيوم) and the source of all nobility and splendor.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ إِلهَكُمْ لَواحِدٌ — "Indeed your God is surely One" (37:4). The address is to all people, and this is the object of the oath (المقسم به). It is a statement presented with a proof, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما وَرَبُّ الْمَشارِقِ — "Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and Lord of the easts" (37:5). This is a predicate after a predicate for the particle inna (إن), or a predicate for an omitted subject — the estimation being "He is the Lord of the heavens..." — or an appositive (بدل) for "One" (واحد).

The presentation of these attributes suggests the reason why the God is One, just as the specific nature of the oath suggests the reason why He is the Lord of the heavens, the earth, and what is between them.

It is as if it were said: Indeed, your God is surely One because the criterion for the divinity of a God — which is His being the rightfully worshipped — is that He be a Lord (رب) who manages affairs, as you yourselves acknowledge. And He, the Glorified, is the Lord of the heavens, the earth, and what is between them, who manages their affairs and disposes of all of them.

And how could it be otherwise? For He, the Exalted, reveals to His Prophet and thereby disposes of the heaven and its inhabitants by sending angels who rank themselves between it and the earth — where the realm of the devils lies — and they repel them. This is His disposal of what is between the heaven and the earth and of the devils. Then they recite the Reminder to His Prophet, which contains the perfection and upbringing of people, whether they believe or deny. Thus, in the revelation, there is His disposal of the heavens, the earth, and what is between them. Therefore, in His oneness, He is the Lord of all, the Manager of their affairs, and the One God.

His saying: وَرَبُّ الْمَشارِقِ — "and Lord of the easts" (37:5) refers to the points of sunrise throughout the different seasons, or it means the points of rising for all stars, or all points of rising in general. In the specific mention of the "easts," there is a suitability to the dawning of revelation with its angels from the heaven. He, the Exalted, has said: وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ بِالْأُفُقِ الْمُبِينِ — "He certainly saw him on the manifest horizon" (81:23), and He said: وَهُوَ بِالْأُفُقِ الْأَعْلى — "while he was on the highest horizon" (53:7).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِزِينَةٍ الْكَواكِبِ — "Indeed We have adorned the lowest heaven with the adornment of the stars" (37:6). By "adornment" (الزينة) is meant that which is used to adorn, and "the stars" (الكواكب) is an explanation (بيان) or an appositive (بدل) for the adornment. The theme of adorning the lowest heaven with the adornment of stars is repeated in His speech, such as His saying: وَزَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ — "and We adorned the lowest heaven with lamps" (41:12), and His saying: وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ — "We have certainly adorned the lowest heaven with lamps" (67:5), and His saying: أَفَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا إِلَى السَّماءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْناها وَزَيَّنَّاها — "Have they not observed the sky above them, how We have built it and adorned it" (50:6).

This carries an apparent indication that the "lowest heaven" (السماء الدنيا) among the seven heavens mentioned in the Quran is the world of stars above the earth, although some have interpreted it in ways that align with the requirements of either ancient or modern astronomy.

His saying, the Exalted: وَحِفْظاً مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطانٍ مارِدٍ — "and [with] a safeguard against every rebellious devil" (37:7). The word "safeguard" (حفظاً) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb, the estimation being: "and We guarded it with a safeguard against every rebellious devil." By "devil" (الشيطان) is meant the evil ones among the jinn, and "rebellious" (المارد) refers to the wicked one who is devoid of any good.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يَسَّمَّعُونَ إِلَى الْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلى وَيُقْذَفُونَ مِنْ كُلِّ جانِبٍ — "They cannot eavesdrop on the Exalted Assembly, and they are pelted from every side" (37:8). The origin of لا يَسَّمَّعُونَ is la yatasamma'un, and التسمع (eavesdropping) means listening intently. It is a metaphor for their being barred and driven away; with this intended meaning, it becomes a description of every devil. If it were taken literally as "listening," the sentence would imply a futility of action, for if they did not listen, there would be no reason to pelt them.

The الملأ (assembly) among people refers to the nobles who "fill the eyes" (يملئون العيون) with their presence. The "Exalted Assembly" (الملأ الأعلى) are those whom the devils wish to eavesdrop upon, namely the noble angels who inhabit the high heavens, as indicated by His speech, such as His saying: لَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ مَلَكاً رَسُولاً — "We would have surely sent down to them an angel from the heaven as an apostle" (17:95).

Their purpose in eavesdropping on the Exalted Assembly is to gain knowledge of the news of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — that is sent down to this earthly world, such as future events and hidden secrets. This is alluded to in His saying: وَما تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ الشَّياطِينُ وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُمْ وَما يَسْتَطِيعُونَ إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ — "The devils have not brought it down: it does not behoove them, nor are they able to [do it]. Indeed they are barred from hearing [it]" (26:210–212), and His saying, narrating from the jinn: وَأَنَّا لَمَسْنَا السَّماءَ فَوَجَدْناها مُلِئَتْ حَرَساً شَدِيداً وَشُهُباً وَأَنَّا كُنَّا نَقْعُدُ مِنْها مَقاعِدَ لِلسَّمْعِ فَمَنْ يَسْتَمِعِ الْآنَ يَجِدْ لَهُ شِهاباً رَصَداً — "Indeed we made for the heaven and found it full of a powerful guard and flaming fires. We used to sit in its observation posts to eavesdrop, but whoever eavesdrops now finds a flaming fire lying in wait for him" (72:8–9).

His saying: وَيُقْذَفُونَ مِنْ كُلِّ جانِبٍ — "and they are pelted from every side" (37:8). القذف (pelting) means throwing, and الجانب (side) means direction.

His saying, the Exalted: دُحُوراً وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ واصِبٌ — "repelled, and for them there is a lasting punishment" (37:9). الدحور (repelling) means driving away and pushing back; it is a verbal noun used in the sense of a passive participle, functioning as a circumstantial qualifier (حال) — meaning "being repelled" — or as an object of purpose (مفعول له) or a cognate accusative. الواصب (lasting) means necessary and perpetual.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ الْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ ثاقِبٌ — "except him who snatches a fragment, whereat there pursues him a piercing flame" (37:10). الخطفة (snatching) means pilfering and seizing quickly. الشهاب (flame) is what is seen in the atmosphere like a falling star. الثقوب (piercing) means penetrating or striking; the flame is called "piercing" because it does not miss its target or goal.

What is meant by "snatching" is the pilfering of hearing, which has been expressed elsewhere as "stealing a hearing" (استراق السمع). Allah, the Exalted, said: إِلَّا مَنِ اسْتَرَقَ السَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ مُبِينٌ — "except him who steals a hearing, whereat there pursues him a manifest flame" (15:18). The exception is from the pronoun of the subject in His saying "they cannot eavesdrop," though some have allowed it to be a disjointed exception (استثناء منقطع).

The meaning of the five verses is: We have adorned the heaven that is closest to you — or the lowest heaven — with an adornment, which is the stars, and We have guarded it with a safeguard against every wicked devil devoid of good, who are barred from listening to the Exalted Assembly — to gain knowledge of the news of the Unseen they exchange among themselves — and they are pelted from every direction while being driven away, and for them is a lasting punishment that never leaves them, except for him who pilfers a snatch of their news, whereat there pursues him a piercing flame that does not miss its mark.

( A Discourse on the Meaning of the Flaming Fires )

The commentators have offered various explanations to conceptualize the devils' stealing of hearing and their being pelted with flaming fires (الشهب). These are based on what immediately comes to mind from the literal sense of the verses and narrations: that there are celestial spheres (أفلاك) surrounding the earth inhabited by groups of angels, having gates through which nothing enters except by them, and that in the first heaven there is a gathering of angels with flaming fires in their hands, lying in wait for the devils who steal a hearing, and then pelting them with these fires.

Today, the falsity of these views has become clear through direct observation. Consequently, the various explanations they provided for the "flaming fires" are also invalidated — explanations they filled in lengthy works such as al-Tafsir al-Kabir by al-Razi, Ruh al-Ma'ani by al-Alusi, and others.

It is possible — and Allah knows best — that these descriptions in His speech are of the nature of parables (الأمثال المضروبة) through which realities beyond the senses are depicted in the form of sensible things to bring them closer to human perception. He, the Mighty and Majestic, is the one who says: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ وَما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ — "We draw these parables for mankind, but no one grasps them except those who possess knowledge" (29:43).

This is frequent in His speech, including the Throne (العرش), the Chair (الكرسي), the Tablet (اللوح), and the Book (الكتاب), as previously indicated and as will be further discussed.

On this basis, the "heaven" inhabited by the angels would refer to a celestial realm (عالم ملكوتي) with a higher horizon, whose relation to this witnessed world is like the relation of the physical heaven with its celestial bodies to the earth. The devils' "approaching the heaven," "stealing a hearing," and "being pelted with flaming fires" would mean their attempt to approach the realm of the angels to gain knowledge of the secrets of creation and future events, and their being repelled by that which they cannot withstand of the light of the celestial realm (نور الملكوت), or their assault upon the Truth to obscure it and the angels' repelling them with the Truth that nullifies their falsehoods.

His mentioning the story of the devils' stealing of hearing and their being pelted with flaming fires immediately after the oath by the angels of revelation and their guarding it from the interference of devils provides some support for what we have mentioned. And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَهُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقاً أَمْ مَنْ خَلَقْنا إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِنْ طِينٍ لازِبٍ — "So ask them: Is their creation more prodigious, or [that of] others We have created? Indeed We created them from a clinging clay" (37:11). اللازب (clinging) means that which sticks together such that it adheres to what is adjacent to it. In Majma' al-Bayan, it is said: al-lazib and al-lazim (the necessary/adhering) are the same in meaning. End quote.

The meaning of His saying مَنْ خَلَقْنا (others We have created) refers either to the angels mentioned in the previous verses — who are the guardians of revelation and the hurlers of the flaming fires — or to other great creations besides humans, such as the heavens, the earth, and the angels. The use of the pronoun for rational beings is for the sake of predominance (التغليب).

The meaning is: If Allah is the Lord of the heavens, the earth, what is between them, and the angels, then ask them to give a formal opinion (يفتوا): Is their creation more prodigious or that of others We have created? For they are the weakest of creation, as We created them from a clinging clay; thus, they cannot frustrate Us.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالصَّافَّاتِ صَفًّا, it is said: "The angels and the prophets."

In the same source, narrated from his father and Ya'qub ibn Yazid, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from some of our companions, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: 'Indeed these stars in the heaven are cities like the cities on the earth...'" (the hadith).

In the same source, in a narration by Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "عَذابٌ واصِبٌ means a permanent, painful punishment that has reached their hearts."

In the same source, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم, in the hadith of the Ascension (المعراج), he said: "Then Gabriel ascended and I ascended with him to the lowest heaven. Over it is an angel called Isma'il, and he is the master of the 'snatch' (صاحب الخطفة) of which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ الْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ ثاقِبٌ. Under him are seventy thousand angels, and under each angel are seventy thousand angels..." (the hadith).

I say: The narrations on this subject are numerous. We have cited some of them in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنِ اسْتَرَقَ السَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ مُبِينٌ (15:18), and some will follow in the commentary on Surat al-Mulk and Surat al-Jinn, if Allah wills.

In Nahj al-Balaghah: "Then He, the Glorified, gathered from the hard and the soft, the sweet and the salt of the earth, clay which He soaked in water until it became pure, and kneaded it with moisture until it became clinging (لزبت)."

Ayahs

بَلْ عَجِبْتَ وَيَسْخَرُونَ (١٢)
Indeed you wonder, while they engage in ridicule, (12)
وَإِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ لَا يَذْكُرُونَ (١٣)
and [even] when admonished do not take admonition, (13)
وَإِذَا رَأَوْا۟ ءَايَةً يَسْتَسْخِرُونَ (١٤)
and when they see a sign they make it an object of ridicule, (14)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
and say, ‘This is nothing but plain magic!’ (15)
أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ (١٦)
‘What! When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected? (16)
أَوَءَابَآؤُنَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (١٧)
And our forefathers too?!’ (17)
قُلْ نَعَمْ وَأَنتُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ (١٨)
Say, ‘Yes! And you will be utterly humble.’ (18)
فَإِنَّمَا هِىَ زَجْرَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ فَإِذَا هُمْ يَنظُرُونَ (١٩)
It will be only a single shout and, behold, they will look on, (19)
وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (٢٠)
and say, ‘Woe to us! This is the Day of Retribution!’ (20)
هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٢١)
‘This is the Day of Judgement that you used to deny!’ (21)
۞ ٱحْشُرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ وَأَزْوَٰجَهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ (٢٢)
‘Muster the wrongdoers and their mates and what they used to worship (22)
مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَٱهْدُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٢٣)
besides Allah, and show them the way to hell! (23)
وَقِفُوهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُم مَّسْـُٔولُونَ (٢٤)
[But first] stop them! For they must be questioned.’ (24)
مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنَاصَرُونَ (٢٥)
‘Why is it that you do not support one another [today]?’ (25)
بَلْ هُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ (٢٦)
‘Indeed, they are [meek and] submissive today!’ (26)
وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٢٧)
Some of them will turn to others, questioning each other. (27)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَأْتُونَنَا عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٢٨)
They will say, ‘Indeed you used to accost us peremptorily.’ (28)
قَالُوا۟ بَل لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٩)
They will answer, ‘No, you [yourselves] had no faith. (29)
وَمَا كَانَ لَنَا عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍۭ ۖ بَلْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا طَـٰغِينَ (٣٠)
We had no authority over you. No, you [yourselves] were a rebellious lot. (30)
فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنَا قَوْلُ رَبِّنَآ ۖ إِنَّا لَذَآئِقُونَ (٣١)
So our Lord’s word became due against us that we shall indeed taste [the punishment]. (31)
فَأَغْوَيْنَـٰكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غَـٰوِينَ (٣٢)
So we perverted you, for we were perverse [ourselves].’ (32)
فَإِنَّهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ (٣٣)
So, that day they will share the punishment. (33)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٣٤)
Indeed, that is how We deal with the guilty. (34)
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوٓا۟ إِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٣٥)
Indeed, it was they who, when they were told, ‘There is no god except Allah,’ used to be disdainful, (35)
وَيَقُولُونَ أَئِنَّا لَتَارِكُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَتِنَا لِشَاعِرٍ مَّجْنُونٍۭ (٣٦)
and [they would] say, ‘Shall we abandon our gods for a crazy poet?’ (36)
بَلْ جَآءَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَصَدَّقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٣٧)
Indeed, he has brought [them] the truth, and confirmed the [earlier] apostles. (37)
إِنَّكُمْ لَذَآئِقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْأَلِيمِ (٣٨)
Indeed you will taste the painful punishment, (38)
وَمَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٣٩)
and you will be requited only for what you used to do (39)
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٤٠)
—[all] except Allah’s exclusive servants. (40)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَّعْلُومٌ (٤١)
For such there is a known provision (41)
فَوَٰكِهُ ۖ وَهُم مُّكْرَمُونَ (٤٢)
—fruits—and they will be held in honour, (42)
فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٤٣)
in the gardens of bliss, (43)
عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (٤٤)
[reclining] on couches, facing one another, (44)
يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِكَأْسٍ مِّن مَّعِينٍۭ (٤٥)
served around with a cup, from a clear fountain, (45)
بَيْضَآءَ لَذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ (٤٦)
snow-white, delicious to the drinkers, (46)
لَا فِيهَا غَوْلٌ وَلَا هُمْ عَنْهَا يُنزَفُونَ (٤٧)
wherein there will be neither headache nor will it cause them stupefaction, (47)
وَعِندَهُمْ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ عِينٌ (٤٨)
and with them will be maidens, of restrained glances with big [beautiful] eyes, (48)
كَأَنَّهُنَّ بَيْضٌ مَّكْنُونٌ (٤٩)
as if they were hidden ostrich eggs. (49)
فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٥٠)
Some of them will turn to others, questioning each other. (50)
قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ إِنِّى كَانَ لِى قَرِينٌ (٥١)
One of them will say, ‘Indeed I had a companion (51)
يَقُولُ أَءِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُصَدِّقِينَ (٥٢)
who used to say, ‘‘Are you really among those who affirm (52)
أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَدِينُونَ (٥٣)
that when we have died and become dust and bones, we shall be brought to retribution?’’ ’ (53)
قَالَ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّطَّلِعُونَ (٥٤)
He will say, ‘Will you have a look?’ (54)
فَٱطَّلَعَ فَرَءَاهُ فِى سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٥٥)
Then he will take a look and sight him in the middle of hell. (55)
قَالَ تَٱللَّهِ إِن كِدتَّ لَتُرْدِينِ (٥٦)
He will say, ‘By Allah, you had almost ruined me! (56)
وَلَوْلَا نِعْمَةُ رَبِّى لَكُنتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ (٥٧)
Had it not been for my Lord’s blessing, I too would have been among those mustered [in hell]!’ (57)
أَفَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ (٥٨)
‘Is it [true] that we shall not die [anymore], (58)
إِلَّا مَوْتَتَنَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ (٥٩)
aside from our earlier death, and that we shall not be punished? (59)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٦٠)
This is indeed the supreme triumph!’ (60)
لِمِثْلِ هَـٰذَا فَلْيَعْمَلِ ٱلْعَـٰمِلُونَ (٦١)
Let all workers work for the like of this! (61)
أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ نُّزُلًا أَمْ شَجَرَةُ ٱلزَّقُّومِ (٦٢)
Is this a better reception, or the Zaqqum tree? (62)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰهَا فِتْنَةً لِّلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٦٣)
Indeed We have made it a punishment for the wrongdoers. (63)
إِنَّهَا شَجَرَةٌ تَخْرُجُ فِىٓ أَصْلِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٦٤)
It is a tree that rises from the depths of hell. (64)
طَلْعُهَا كَأَنَّهُۥ رُءُوسُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ (٦٥)
Its spathes are as if they were devils’ heads. (65)
فَإِنَّهُمْ لَـَٔاكِلُونَ مِنْهَا فَمَالِـُٔونَ مِنْهَا ٱلْبُطُونَ (٦٦)
They will eat from it and gorge with it their bellies. (66)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ لَهُمْ عَلَيْهَا لَشَوْبًا مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ (٦٧)
On top of that they will take a solution of scalding water. (67)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ مَرْجِعَهُمْ لَإِلَى ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٦٨)
Then their retreat will be toward hell. (68)
إِنَّهُمْ أَلْفَوْا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ ضَآلِّينَ (٦٩)
They had found their fathers astray, (69)
فَهُمْ عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِمْ يُهْرَعُونَ (٧٠)
yet they press onwards in their footsteps. (70)

(Commentary)

This section recounts their mockery of the signs of Allah (آيات الله) and some of their sayings based on disbelief (الكفر) and the denial of the Resurrection (المعاد). It provides a rebuttal to them by affirming the matter of the Resurrection (البعث), the severity and various types of punishment (العذاب) that will befall them therein, and the blessings (النعمة) and honor (الكرامة) with which Allah favors His devoted servants (عباده المخلصين).

It also mentions the disputation of the people of the Fire on the Day of Resurrection, a conversation between the people of Paradise, and another between some of them and some of the people of the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ عَجِبْتَ وَيَسْخَرُونَ وَإِذا ذُكِّرُوا لا يَذْكُرُونَ — Rather, you are astonished, and they mock. When they are reminded, they do not take heed. (37:12-13). That is: Rather, you were astonished, O Muhammad, at their denial of you despite your calling them to the word of truth (كلمة الحق), while they mock and ridicule your astonishment at them or your calling them to the truth. When they are reminded of the signs of Allah (آيات الله) that point toward monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the religion of truth (دين الحق), they do not take heed nor do they pay attention.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَأَوْا آيَةً يَسْتَسْخِرُونَ — And when they see a sign, they mock. (37:14). In Majma‘ al-Bayan, it is stated: "Sakhira (سخر) and istasakhara (استسخر) carry the same meaning." End quote.

The meaning is: Whenever these polytheists (المشركون) see any miracle among the miraculous signs of Allah (آيات الله المعجزة), such as the Quran or the splitting of the moon, they mock it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ — And say, ‘This is nothing but plain magic.’ (37:15). In their pointing to the sign with the word "this" (هذا), there is an indication that they perceive nothing from it except that it is "some thing" without further regard, which is among the strongest forms of insult and mockery (الاستسخار).

His saying, the Exalted: أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ أَوَآباؤُنَا الْأوَّلُونَ — ‘What! When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected? And our forefathers too?’ (37:16-17). This is a denial of the Resurrection (البعث) — the raising of the dead for judgment — based on deeming it improbable (الاستبعاد). For the imagination (الوهم) deems it improbable that a human should die, his body disintegrate and return to dust and bones, and then return to his original form.

Evidence that the speech is intended to convey improbability (الاستبعاد) is their repetition of the interrogative of denial regarding their early forefathers; for the imagination's deeming it improbable that they should be resurrected—when their traces have been erased and nothing remains of them but stories—is far more intense and powerful than its deeming their own resurrection improbable.

Had their denial of the Resurrection been based on the premise that they become non-existent (ينعدمون) upon death, making their restoration impossible (تستحيل إعادتهم), the judgment regarding them and their forefathers would have been the same, and there would have been no need to renew the interrogation regarding their forefathers.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ نَعَمْ وَأَنْتُمْ داخِرُونَ فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَإِذا هُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ — Say, ‘Yes! And you will be utterly humbled.’ It will be only a single shout, and then, behold, they will be looking on. (37:18-19). Allah commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to answer them that they shall indeed be resurrected.

His saying: وَأَنْتُمْ داخِرُونَ — And you will be utterly humbled — means belittled, insulted, and abased. This is, in reality, an argument based on the universality of Power (القدرة) and the efficacy of Will (الإرادة) without delay; for His command, when He wills a thing, is only that He says to it "Be," and it is. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَإِذا هُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ — It will be only a single shout, and then, behold, they will be looking on. Allah has said: وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. The matter of the Hour is just like the twinkling of an eye, or even swifter. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (16:77).

His saying: فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ — It will be only a single shout — the "fa" (ف) serves to provide a causal explanation (التعليل), and the sentence explains the reason for His saying: وَأَنْتُمْ داخِرُونَ — And you will be utterly humbled. The use of the word "shout" (زجرة) suggests their humiliation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا يا وَيْلَنا هذا يَوْمُ الدِّينِ هذا يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ — They will say, ‘Woe to us! This is the Day of Retribution.’ ‘This is the Day of Judgment which you used to deny.’ (37:20-21). This is coordinated with His saying: يَنْظُرُونَ — they will be looking on — which suggests they are stunned, bewildered, and reflecting. Then they realize it is the Day of Resurrection (يوم البعث) wherein there is religion (الدين) and recompense (الجزاء), and they are wary of it because of their disbelief and denial. Thus they said: "the Day of Retribution" (يوم الدين), and did not say "the Day of Resurrection." The use of the past tense indicates the certainty of its occurrence.

His saying: هذا يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ — This is the Day of Judgment which you used to deny. It is said this is the speech of some of them to others, and it is said it is the speech of the angels or the speech of Allah, the Exalted, to them; the following verse supports this. "The Judgment" (الفصل) is the distinguishing between two things, and it is called the Day of Judgment (يوم الفصل) because it is the day of distinguishing between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل) by the decree and judgment of Allah, the Exalted, or the distinguishing between the guilty (المجرمين) and the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness and protect themselves against evil. Allah, the Exalted, said: وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ — ‘Get apart today, O guilty ones!’ (36:59).

His saying, the Exalted: احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَأَزْواجَهُمْ وَما كانُوا يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَاهْدُوهُمْ إِلى صِراطِ الْجَحِيمِ — [The angels will be told,] ‘Muster the wrongdoers and their mates and what they used to worship besides Allah, and guide them to the path of hell. (37:22-23). This is from the speech of Allah, the Exalted, to the angels. The meaning is: We said to the angels: "Muster them." It is also said that it is the speech of the angels to one another.

The Mustering (الحشر) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is the removal of a group from their place and driving them from it toward war or the like.

What is meant by "those who did wrong" (الذين ظلموا), as supported by the end of the verse, are the polytheists (المشركون); not all polytheists, but the stubborn opponents of the truth who bar others from it. Allah, the Exalted, said: فَأذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ — Thereupon a caller will announce in their midst, ‘May Allah’s curse be on the wrongdoers! —those who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter.’ (7:44-45). The use of the past tense in this context serves the purpose of a description (الوصف); thus, "those who did wrong" does not refer to anyone from whom any wrong (ظلم) occurred even once, but rather it is a definition of them by the sum of what they earned in their worldly life. It is as if it were asked: "What did so-and-so do in his life?" and it is said: "He did wrong." Thus, the verb serves as a description. In the speech of Allah, the Exalted, there is much of this, such as His saying: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ زُمَراً — Those who were wary of their Lord will be led to paradise in throngs (39:73), and His saying: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً — The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs (39:71), and His saying: لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنى وَزيادَةٌ — For those who do good there will be the best reward and more (10:26).

His saying: وَأَزْواجَهُمْ — and their mates. It appears that what is meant are their companions (قرناؤهم) from the devils (الشياطين). Allah, the Exalted, said: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ — We assign a devil to be the companion of him who turns a blind eye to the remembrance of the All-beneficent... until He said: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَنا قالَ يا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ — ...until when he comes to Us, he says [to his companion], ‘I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion!’ (43:36-38).

It is also said that "mates" (الأزواج) refers to their likes and counterparts; thus, the practitioners of adultery are mustered with the practitioners of adultery, the drinkers of wine with the drinkers of wine, and so on.

The problem with this is that it necessitates that "those who did wrong" refers to a specific group from the practitioners of every sin, which the wording does not support, nor does the end of the verse suit it.

It is also said that "mates" refers to their disbelieving wives, but this is weak like the previous view.

His saying: وَما كانُوا يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — and what they used to worship besides Allah. It appears that what is meant are the idols they worshipped, given the literal sense of the word "what" (ما). Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ — Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell (21:98).

It is possible that the word "what" (ما) includes rational beings among the worshipped, such as the Pharaohs and Nimrods. As for the worshipped angels and the Messiah (peace be upon him), they are excluded from the generality by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ — Indeed those to whom the [promise of] the best reward has gone forth from Us, they shall be kept far from it. (21:101).

His saying: فَاهْدُوهُمْ إِلى صِراطِ الْجَحِيمِ — and guide them to the path of hell. The Blazing Fire (الجحيم) is one of the names of Hell (جهنم) in the Quran, and it is derived from al-jahmah (الجحمة), meaning the intensity of the fire's flaming, according to what al-Raghib mentioned.

What is meant by "guiding them" to its path is bringing them to it and casting them into it by driving them. It is said that calling this "guidance" (هداية) is a form of mockery. In Majma‘ al-Bayan, it is stated: "It was expressed as guidance only because it was a substitute for guidance to Paradise, just as in His saying: فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ — so give them the news of a painful punishment — since this 'good news' occurred for them as a substitute for the good news of bliss." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ ما لَكُمْ لا تَناصَرُونَ بَلْ هُمُ الْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ — And stop them, for they are to be questioned: ‘Why is it that you do not help one another?’ Rather, today they are [utterly] submissive. (37:24-26). In al-Majma‘, it is said: "It is said: waqaftu ana (I stopped) and waqaftu ghayri (I stopped another) — meaning it is both intransitive and transitive — and some of Banu Tamim say: awqaftu al-dabbah (I stopped the beast)." End quote.

So His saying: وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ — And stop them, for they are to be questioned — means detain them because they are to be questioned, i.e., until they are asked. The context suggests that this command to stop and the questioning occur on the path to the Blazing Fire.

Opinions differed regarding what they are questioned about. It is said: they are questioned about the statement "There is no god but Allah." It is said: about drinking cold water, as a way of mocking them. It is said: about the mastership (ولاية) of Ali (peace be upon him).

These views, if correct, only point to instances of what is questioned. The context bears witness that the questioning is what is contained in His saying: ما لَكُمْ لا تَناصَرُونَ — Why is it that you do not help one another? — meaning, why does one of you not help the other as you used to do in the world, seeking assistance for your needs and goals? What follows it, His saying: بَلْ هُمُ الْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ — Rather, today they are [utterly] submissive — meaning they are surrendered and do not act arrogantly, indicates that what is meant by His saying: ما لَكُمْ لا تَناصَرُونَ is the questioning about their arrogance (استكبار) against obeying the truth, just as they were arrogant in the world.

Thus, the questioning about their lack of mutual help is a questioning about the cause of the arrogance they were upon in the world. It has thereby become clear that what is questioned is every truth they turned away from in the world, whether a true belief or a righteous deed (عمل صالح), out of arrogance against the truth and a display of mutual support.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ — And they will turn to one another, questioning each other... until His saying: إِنَّا كُنَّا غاوِينَ — ...we were indeed perverse. (37:27-32). This is a disputation occurring between the followers (الأتباع) and the followed (المتبوعين) on the Day of Resurrection. It is expressed as "questioning each other" (تساؤل) because it carries the meaning of them asking one another in blame and reproach. The followers say to those they followed: "Why did you mislead us?" and the followed say: "Why did you accept from us when we had no authority over you?"

So His saying: وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ — the first "some" are the objectors, and the second "some" are those objected against, as indicated by the context of the questioning; and their questioning is their disputation.

His saying: قالُوا إِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَأْتُونَنا عَنِ الْيَمِينِ — They will say, ‘Indeed you used to come to us from the right.’ (37:28). That is, from the direction of good and happiness (السعادة); the use of "the right" (اليمين) for this is common and frequent, as in His saying: وَأَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ ما أَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ — And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand? (56:27). The meaning is that you used to come to us from the direction of good and happiness, blocking the path and intervening between us and good and happiness, and misleading us.

It is said: what is meant by "the right" is religion (الدين), which is close to the previous view. It is also said: what is meant by "the right" is coercion and power, as in His saying, the Exalted: فَراغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْباً بِالْيَمِينِ — Then he turned upon them, striking [them] with the right hand (37:93); this is not without merit considering the response of the followed.

His saying: قالُوا بَلْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ وَما كانَ لَنا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ — They will say, ‘Rather, you [yourselves] were not faithful. We had no authority over you...’ until His saying: غاوِينَ — ...perverse. (37:29-32). This is the response of the followed, exonerating themselves from the wretchedness of the followers and stating that their crime is attributed to their own evil choice.

They said: بَلْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ — Rather, you [yourselves] were not faithful — meaning, we were not the necessitating cause for your crime and destruction by your being devoid of faith; rather, you were not believers, not that we stripped you of faith.

Then they said: وَما كانَ لَنا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ — We had no authority over you — which is in the sense of a response for the sake of argument. It is as if it were said: Even if it were assumed that you had faith, we had no authority (سلطان) over you such that we could seize it from you and strip you of it. Moreover, the authority of the followed is only through the followers; they are the ones who give them power and strength, so they gain authority over them.

Then they said: بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ — Rather, you were a rebellious people. Rebellion (الطغيان) is the exceeding of the limit. This is a transition (إضراب) from the saying: "you were not faithful." It is as if it were said: The cause of your destruction was not merely the absence of faith; rather, you were a rebellious people just as we were arrogant and rebellious. So we all cooperated in abandoning the path of rectitude (الرشد) and taking the path of perversity (الغي). Thus, the word of punishment (كلمة العذاب) which Allah, the Glorified, decreed has become due against us. Allah, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ كانَتْ مِرْصاداً لِلطَّاغِينَ مَآباً — Indeed hell is an ambush, a resort for the rebellious (78:21-22), and He said: فَأَمَّا مَنْ طَغى وَآثَرَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا فَإِنَّ الْجَحِيمَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى — As for him who was rebellious and preferred the life of this world, hell will indeed be [his] abode. (79:37-39).

For this reason, He followed the saying: بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ with His saying: فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنا قَوْلُ رَبِّنا إِنَّا لَذائِقُونَ — So our Lord’s word has become due against us: we shall indeed taste [the punishment]. (37:31). That is, we shall taste the punishment.

Then they said: فَأَغْوَيْناكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غاوِينَ — So we misled you, for we were ourselves perverse. (37:32). This is branched (متفرع) from the establishment of the word of punishment and the finality of the causes for their destruction; for rebellion (الطغيان) entails perversity (الغواية) and then the fire of Hell. Allah, the Exalted, said to Iblis: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — ‘As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all.’ (16:42-43).

It is as if it were said: When you clothed yourselves in rebellion, perversity befell you at our hands without us having any authority over you except your following us and your connection to us. Thus, the attribute that was in us—namely perversity—spread to you. For from the perverse (الغاوي) nothing proceeds but perversity, and a vessel only leaks what is in it. In short, you were not coerced nor was your choice (الاختيار) stripped from you from the moment you began walking the path of destruction until you fell into its abyss, which is perversity. Thus, the Word has become due against you.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ فِي الْعَذابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ — So that day they will [all] share in the punishment... until His saying: يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ — ...they used to be arrogant. (37:33-35). The pronoun in "they" (فَإِنَّهُمْ) refers to both the followers and the followed; they share in the punishment because of their sharing in wrongdoing (الظلم) and their cooperation in crime (الجرم), without any preference for some over others.

Some have inferred that the misleaders (المغوين) suffer a more severe punishment, and that is in proportion to their own burdens and burdens like their own burdens. Thus, sharing (الشركة) does not necessitate equality. The truth is that the verses are intended to state their sharing in wrongdoing, crime, and the punishment that follows them because of it. It is possible, nonetheless, that there befall each of the followed and the followers types of punishment arising from the specifics of their state. Allah, the Exalted, said: وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَأَثْقالاً مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ — They will surely carry their own burdens, and [other] burdens along with their own burdens (29:13), and He said: رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ أَضَلُّونا فَآتِهِمْ عَذاباً ضِعْفاً مِنَ النَّارِ قالَ لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلكِنْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ — ‘Our Lord, it was they who misled us; so give them a double punishment of the Fire.’ He will say, ‘For each there is a double [punishment], but you do not know.’ (7:38).

His saying: إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِالْمُجْرِمِينَ — Indeed that is how We deal with the guilty. (37:34). This is an emphasis on the realization of the punishment. What is meant by "the guilty" (المجرمين) are the polytheists, as evidenced by His saying afterward: إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللهُ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ — Indeed when they were told, ‘There is no god but Allah,’ they used to be arrogant. (37:35). That is, when monotheism (التوحيد) was presented to them so they might believe in it, or the word of sincerity (كلمة الإخلاص) so they might say it, they persisted in their arrogance and did not accept.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ أَإِنَّا لَتارِكُوا آلِهَتِنا لِشاعِرٍ مَجْنُونٍ بَلْ جاءَ بِالْحَقِّ وَصَدَّقَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — And say, ‘Shall we abandon our gods for a crazy poet?’ Rather, he has brought the truth and confirmed the apostles. (37:36-37). This saying of theirs is a denial of the Messengership (الرسالة) after their arrogance against monotheism and their denial of it.

His saying: بَلْ جاءَ بِالْحَقِّ وَصَدَّقَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — Rather, he has brought the truth and confirmed the apostles — is a rebuttal to their saying: "for a crazy poet," where they accused him (peace be upon him) of poetry and madness. In this, there is an accusation against the Book of Allah of being poetry and the ramblings of madness. So He refuted them by stating that what he brought is truth, and in it is the confirmation of the previous Messengers. Thus, it is not a false speech like poetry or the rambling of madness, nor is it an unprecedented innovation (بدع) in its meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ لَذائِقُوا الْعَذابِ الْأَلِيمِ — You will indeed taste the painful punishment. (37:38). This is a threat to them of punishment for their arrogance and their accusing the truth of being falsehood.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — And you will be requited only for what you used to do. (37:39). That is, there is no injustice in it because it is your very deed being returned to you.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — [All will be punished] except the chosen servants of Allah... until His saying: بَيْضٌ مَكْنُونٌ — ...as if they were [delicate] hidden eggs. (37:40-49). This is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع) from the pronoun in "tasters" (لَذائِقُوا) or from the pronoun in "you will be requited" (تُجْزَوْنَ), and each has a basis. According to the first, the meaning is: But the devoted servants of Allah, those shall have a known provision and they are not tasters of the painful punishment. According to the second, the meaning is: But the devoted servants of Allah, those shall have a known provision beyond the recompense for their deeds; an indication of its meaning will follow.

The possibility of the exception being connected (متصل) is weak and not without affectation.

Allah, the Glorified, has called them "the devoted servants of Allah" (عباد الله المخلصين), thus affirming for them the servitude (العبودية) to Himself. The servant (العبد) is he who possesses nothing for himself, neither will nor action; so these do not will except what Allah wills and do not act except for Him.

Then He affirmed for them that they are "chosen" (مخلصين) with a fathah on the lam, meaning that Allah, the Exalted, has chosen them for Himself, so no one shares them with Him. They have no attachment to anything other than Him, the Exalted—neither from the adornment of the worldly life nor from the blessings of the Hereafter—and there is nothing in their hearts but Allah, the Glorified.

It is well known that one whose attribute is such, his pleasure and enjoyment are different from what others enjoy, and his provision is different from what others are provided with, even if he shares with them the necessities of food and drink. From here, it is supported that what is meant by His saying: أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَعْلُومٌ — For them there is a known provision — is an indication that their provision in Paradise—as they are devoted servants—is a special provision that does not resemble the provision of others and is not mixed with what those below them enjoy, even if they share the name.

So His saying: أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَعْلُومٌ means a special, designated provision, distinguished from the provision of others. Its being "known" (معلوم) is a metaphor for its distinction, as in His saying: وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ — There is none of us but has a known station (37:164). The use of the distal demonstrative (أُولئِكَ) is to indicate the loftiness of their station.

As for what some have interpreted—that their provision being "known" means its characteristics are known, such as being neither cut off nor forbidden, beautiful in appearance, delicious in taste, and pleasant in scent—and likewise what others mentioned—that it means it is known in time because of His saying: وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيها بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا — and they will have their provision therein morning and evening (19:62)—and likewise the saying of the one who said it means Paradise: these are all unsound views.

From this, it appears that taking His saying: إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ as an exception from the pronoun in وَما تُجْزَوْنَ is not without merit, as previously indicated.

His saying: فَواكِهُ وَهُمْ مُكْرَمُونَ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ — Fruits; and they will be held in honor in the gardens of bliss. (37:42-43). Fruits (الفواكه) is the plural of fakihah, which refers to the fruits one enjoys; it is an explanation of their "known provision," except that He, the Exalted, coupled it with His saying: وَهُمْ مُكْرَمُونَ — and they will be held in honor — to indicate the distinction of this provision—namely the fruit—from what others have, in that it is accompanied by a special honoring that is specific to them, corresponding to their being specific to Allah, the Glorified, and His being for them with nothing sharing Him in them.

In the annexation of "gardens" to "bliss" (جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ), there is an indication of this. It has already been mentioned regarding His saying: فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ — they will be with those whom Allah has blessed (4:69), and His saying: وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي — and I have completed My blessing upon you (5:3), and others, that the reality of blessing (النعمة) is the Mastership (الولاية) — the divine guardianship and authority — which is His being, the Exalted, the one who manages the affairs of His servant.

His saying: عَلى سُرُرٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ — on couches, facing one another. (37:44). Couches (السرر) is the plural of sarir, which is well known. Their being "facing one another" means their intimacy with one another and their enjoyment of looking into one another's faces, without any of them seeing the back of another.

His saying: يُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِكَأْسٍ مِنْ مَعِينٍ — They will be served with a cup from a flowing spring. (37:45). The cup (الكأس) is the vessel for drink. It is reported from many lexicographers that a drink vessel is not called a ka’s unless it contains drink; if it is empty, it is a qadah. The flowing spring (المعين) of drink is that which is visible, from ‘ana al-ma’ when it appears and flows on the surface of the earth. What is meant by the cup being from a flowing spring is the clarity of the drink within it; therefore, He followed it with His saying: بَيْضاءَ — white.

His saying: بَيْضاءَ لَذَّةٍ لِلشَّارِبِينَ — white, delicious to the drinkers. (37:46). That is, clear in its whiteness, delicious to those who drink. "Deliciousness" (لذة) is a verbal noun used as a description for emphasis, or it is the feminine of ladhdh, meaning delicious, as has been said.

His saying: لا فِيها غَوْلٌ وَلا هُمْ عَنْها يُنْزَفُونَ — It will neither cause any hang-over, nor will they be intoxicated by it. (37:47). The hang-over/harm (الغول) is harm and corruption. Al-Raghib said: al-ghawl is the destruction of a thing from where it is not perceived. End quote. So the negation of ghawl from the wine is the negation of its harms. Intoxication (الإنزاف) has been interpreted as the drunkenness that takes away the intellect, and its root is the gradual removal of a thing.

The sum of the meaning is: It does not contain the harms of the wine that is in the world, nor its intoxication by taking away the intellect.

His saying: وَعِنْدَهُمْ قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ عِينٌ — And with them will be maidens of modest gaze, with large, lovely eyes. (37:48). A description of the Huris with which they are provided. The "modesty of their gaze" (قصور طرفهن) is a metaphor for their looking with a gaze of coquetry and allure; this is supported by the mention of large eyes (العين) after it, which is the plural of ‘ayna’, the feminine of a‘yan, referring to one with wide, beautiful eyes.

It is also said: what is meant by "maidens of modest gaze" is that they restrict their gaze to their husbands, desiring no others due to their love for them; and by "large eyes" that their eyes are intense in their blackness and intense in their whiteness.

His saying: كَأَنَّهُنَّ بَيْضٌ مَكْنُونٌ — as if they were [delicate] hidden eggs. (37:49). Eggs (البيض) are well known; it is a generic noun whose singular is baydah. Hidden (المكنون) is that which is covered by being stored away. It is said: the meaning is to liken them to eggs covered by feathers in the nest or elsewhere, so that hands have not touched them nor has dust reached them. It is also said: the meaning is to liken them to the inner part of the egg before it is peeled and before hands touch it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ — Then they will turn to one another, questioning each other... until His saying: فَلْيَعْمَلِ الْعامِلُونَ — ...let the workers work. (37:50-61). A recount of a conversation that takes place between the people of Paradise, wherein some ask about the states of others, and some recount what happened to them in the world. The conversation ends with them speaking to some of the people of the Fire while he is in the midst of the Blazing Fire.

So His saying: فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ — the plural pronoun refers to the people of Paradise from the devoted servants of Allah. Their "questioning each other" — as previously mentioned — is the asking of some about others and what happened to them.

His saying: قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّي كانَ لِي قَرِينٌ — One of them will say, ‘Indeed I had a companion.’ (37:51). That is, a speaker among the questioning people of Paradise said: "Indeed, I had in the world a close associate among people." This is what the context suggests.

It is said: what is meant by "companion" (القرين) is the companion from the devils. The problem with this is that the Quran only establishes companions from the devils for those who turn away from the remembrance of Allah, while the devoted (المخلصون) are in divine protection (عصمة) from the companion of the devils, as well as from the devil's influence over them, as recounted from Iblis regarding his exception of them from misleading: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your chosen servants among them.’ (38:82-83). Yes, it is perhaps possible for the devil to confront them without having influence over them, but that is not the effect of a "companion" (القرين).

His saying: يَقُولُ أَإِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُصَدِّقِينَ أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَدِينُونَ — who used to say, ‘‘Are you really one of those who believe [in the resurrection]? What! When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we really be judged?’’ (37:52-53). The pronoun in "he says" (يَقُولُ) refers to the companion. The object of "those who believe" (الْمُصَدِّقِينَ) is the Resurrection for recompense, and its place has been taken by His saying: أَإِذا مِتْنا etc. The judged (المدينون) are those who are recompensed.

The meaning is: My companion used to say to me, deeming it improbable and denying it: "Are you really among those who believe in the Resurrection for recompense? When we have died and become dust and bones, and our bodies have disintegrated and their forms changed, shall we really be recompensed by being brought to life and restored?" This is something that ought not to be believed.

His saying: قالَ هَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُطَّلِعُونَ — He will say, ‘Would you like to take a look?’ (37:54). The pronoun in "he said" (قالَ) refers to the aforementioned speaker. Taking a look (الاطلاع) is to peer over. The meaning is: Then the aforementioned speaker said, addressing his interlocutors among the people of Paradise: "Are you peering over the Fire so that you may see my companion and the state he is in?"

His saying: فَاطَّلَعَ فَرَآهُ فِي سَواءِ الْجَحِيمِ — Then he will take a look and see him in the middle of hell. (37:55). The middle (السواء) is the center; from it is sawa’ al-tariq, meaning the middle of the road. The meaning is: The aforementioned speaker peered over the Fire and saw him—i.e., his companion—in the middle of the Blazing Fire.

His saying: قالَ تَاللهِ إِنْ كِدْتَ لَتُرْدِينِ — He will say, ‘By Allah, you had almost ruined me!’ (37:56). The "in" (إِنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy inna. Ruining (الإرداء) is falling from a high place, like a precipice, and it is used as a metaphor for destruction. The meaning is: I swear by Allah, you nearly destroyed me and made me fall into that which you have fallen into of the Blazing Fire.

His saying: وَلَوْ لا نِعْمَةُ رَبِّي لَكُنْتُ مِنَ الْمُحْضَرِينَ — Had it not been for my Lord’s blessing, I would have surely been one of those who are hauled [into hell]. (37:57). What is meant by "blessing" (النعمة) is divine success (التوفيق) and guidance. Being hauled (الإحضار) is being brought for punishment. It is said in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "Ahdara (to haul/bring) is not used absolutely except in evil."

The meaning is: Had it not been for my Lord's success and His guidance, I would have been among those hauled for punishment like you.

His saying: أَفَما نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتَنَا الْأُولى وَما نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ — ‘Are we not to die [any more], except for our first death, and we shall not be punished?’ (37:58-59). The interrogation is for affirmation (التقرير) and wonder (التعجيب). What is meant by the "first death" is the death from the worldly life. As for the death from the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — indicated by His saying: رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ — ‘Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life’ (40:11), he paid no heed to it because the death in which the claimant alleges annihilation and futility is the worldly death.

The meaning—given the omission and brevity in the speech—is: Then the aforementioned speaker returns to himself and his companions and says in wonder: "Are we to be eternal and blessed, such that we are not to die except for the first death, and we are not to be punished?"

It is said in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "They intend by this realization, not doubt. They only said this because they have in it a renewed joy and a doubled happiness, even if they already knew they would abide eternally in Paradise. This is like a man who is given much wealth and says in wonder: 'Is all this wealth mine?' while he knows that it is his." This is like the saying:

Is this the valley of Mecca that I see with my own eyes, and is this I?

He said: "For this reason, He followed it with His saying: إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ — This is indeed the great triumph." End quote.

His saying: إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ — This is indeed the great triumph (37:60) — is part of the speech of the aforementioned speaker, and in it is an exaltation of the gift of eternity and the removal of punishment, and a gratitude for the blessing.

His saying: لِمِثْلِ هذا فَلْيَعْمَلِ الْعامِلُونَ — For the like of this let the workers work. (37:61). The apparent context is that it is from the speech of the aforementioned speaker, and the pointing with "this" is to the triumph or the reward. That is: for the like of this triumph or reward, let the workers work in the abode of obligation (دار التكليف). It is also said: it is from the speech of Allah, the Glorified; and it is said: from the speech of the people of Paradise.

Know that there are various opinions regarding the attribution of most of the preceding sentences to the speech of Allah, the speech of the angels, or the speech of the people of Paradise other than the aforementioned speaker. What we have presented is what the context supports.

His saying, the Exalted: أَذلِكَ خَيْرٌ نُزُلاً أَمْ شَجَرَةُ الزَّقُّومِ — Is this better as a hospitality, or the tree of Zaqqum? ... until His saying: يُهْرَعُونَ — ...they are being rushed. (37:62-70). A comparison between what Allah has prepared as hospitality (نزل) for the people of Paradise from the noble provision He described, and what He has prepared as hospitality for the people of the Fire from the tree of Zaqqum, whose fruit is as if it were the heads of devils, and a drink of boiling water.

So His saying: أَذلِكَ خَيْرٌ نُزُلاً أَمْ شَجَرَةُ الزَّقُّومِ — the pointing with "this" (ذلِكَ) is to the noble provision mentioned previously, prepared for the arrival of the people of Paradise. Hospitality (النزل) refers to what is prepared for the arrival of a guest and presented to him when he arrives, such as fruits and the like.

Zaqqum (الزقوم) — according to what has been said — is the name of a tree with small leaves, bitter and of foul odor, possessing a milky sap that causes swelling if it touches a human body; it is found in Tihamah and the barren lands adjacent to the desert. The tree described with the attributes in the verse was named after it. It is said that the Quraysh did not know it; this will be discussed in the traditions.

The word "better" (خير) in the verse is in the sense of a description rather than a preference, since there is no goodness (خيرية) in Zaqqum at all. It is like His saying: ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ — what is with Allah is better than diversion (62:11). The verse, as the context suggests, is from the speech of Allah, the Exalted.

His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْناها فِتْنَةً لِلظَّالِمِينَ — We have made it a punishment for the wrongdoers. (37:63). The pronoun refers to the tree of Zaqqum. Punishment (الفتنة) means trial and torment.

His saying: إِنَّها شَجَرَةٌ تَخْرُجُ فِي أَصْلِ الْجَحِيمِ — It is a tree that grows from the bottom of hell. (37:64). A description of the tree of Zaqqum. The "bottom" (أصل) of the Blazing Fire is its floor. There is no wonder in a tree growing in fire and remaining within it; for the life of a human and his remaining eternal within it is more wondrous, and Allah does what He wills.

His saying: طَلْعُها كَأَنَّهُ رُؤُسُ الشَّياطِينِ — Its fruit is as if it were the heads of devils. (37:65). The fruit (الطلع) is the produce of the palm tree or any tree when it first appears. The likening of the fruit of Zaqqum to the heads of devils is with the consideration that common imaginations picture the devil in the ugliest form, just as they picture the angel in the best and most beautiful form. Allah, the Exalted, said: ما هذا بَشَراً إِنْ هذا إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ — ‘This is not a human being! This is nothing but a noble angel!’ (12:31). By this, the objection is refuted that a thing is only likened to what is known, and no one has knowledge of the heads of devils.

His saying: فَإِنَّهمْ لَآكِلُونَ مِنْها فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ — They will surely eat from it and fill [their] bellies with it. (37:66). The "fa" (ف) is for causal explanation, explaining its being hospitality for the wrongdoers who eat from it. In His saying: فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ — and fill [their] bellies with it — there is an indication of the mastery of an intense hunger over them, by which they are eager to eat however it may be.

His saying: ثُمَّ إِنَّ لَهُمْ عَلَيْها لَشَوْباً مِنْ حَمِيمٍ — Then they shall have a mixture of boiling water on top of it. (37:67). The mixture (الشوب) is the blend and compound. Boiling water (الحميم) is hot water that has reached the extreme of its heat. The meaning is: Then those wrongdoers shall have—in addition to it—a blend mixed from hot water of extreme heat, which they drink, and it mixes with what they filled their bellies with of Zaqqum.

His saying: ثُمَّ إِنَّ مَرْجِعَهُمْ لَإِلَى الْجَحِيمِ — Then their return shall indeed be to hell. (37:68). That is, after drinking the boiling water, they return to the Blazing Fire, where they settle and are punished. In the verse, there is an allusion that the boiling water is outside the Blazing Fire.

His saying: إِنَّهُمْ أَلْفَوْا آباءَهُمْ ضالِّينَ فَهُمْ عَلى آثارِهِمْ يُهْرَعُونَ — They found their fathers astray, so they are being rushed in their footsteps. (37:69-70). I found (ألفيت) such a thing, meaning I found it and encountered it. Rushing (الإهراع) is hurrying. The meaning is that the cause of their eating and drinking and then returning to the Blazing Fire is that they encountered their fathers astray—and they were imitators and followers of them, and they were their root and reference—so they hurry in their footsteps. Thus, they were recompensed with such hospitality, and the return to the Blazing Fire is a fitting recompense.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Jurayj: regarding His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ عَجِبْتَ — Rather, you are astonished — he said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'I was astonished by the Quran when it was revealed, while the misguided among the children of Adam mock it.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا — Muster the wrongdoers — he said: "Those who wronged the family of Muhammad عليه‌السلام regarding their right." وَأَزْواجَهُمْ — and their mates — he said: "Their likes."

I say: The beginning of the narration is of the type of application (الجري).

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ — And stop them, for they are to be questioned — it is said: "About the mastership (ولاية) of Ali عليه‌السلام" — narrated from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri.

I say: It was also narrated by al-Shaykh in al-Amali, with his chain to Anas ibn Malik from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; and in al-‘Uyun, from Ali and from al-Rida عليه‌السلام from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; and in the Tafsir of al-Qummi, from the Imam (ع).

In al-Khisal, from Amir al-Mu’minin عليه‌السلام, he said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'The feet of a servant shall not move on the Day of Resurrection until he is questioned about four things: about his life, how he spent it; about his youth, how he wore it out; about his wealth, from where he earned it and on what he spent it; and about the love for us, the People of the House (أهل البيت).'"

I say: A similar narration was reported in al-‘Ilal from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In Nahj al-Balaghah: "Fear Allah regarding His servants and His lands, for you are to be questioned even about the plots of land and the cattle."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari narrated in his Tarikh, and al-Tirmidhi, al-Darimi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim, and Ibn Mardawayh from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'There is no caller who calls to a thing except that he will be stopped on the Day of Resurrection, held to it and not parting from it—even if a man calls another man.' Then he recited: وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ."

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Madani from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in a hadith: "As for His saying: أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَعْلُومٌ, he said: 'The servants (الخدام) know it, so they bring it to the friends of Allah before they ask them for it.' As for His saying: فَواكِهُ وَهُمْ مُكْرَمُونَ, he said: 'They do not desire anything in Paradise except that they are honored with it.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: "فَاطَّلَعَ فَرَآهُ فِي سَواءِ الْجَحِيمِ — he says: 'In the middle of the Blazing Fire.'"

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَفَما نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ etc., with its chain to his father from Ali ibn Mahziyar and al-Hasan ibn Mahbub from al-Nadr ibn Suwayd from Durust from Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, who said: "When the people of Paradise enter Paradise and the people of the Fire enter the Fire, Death is brought and slaughtered like a ram between Paradise and the Fire. Then it is said: 'Eternity, so no death ever!' Then the people of Paradise say: أَفَما نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتَنَا الْأُولى وَما نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ ـ إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ ـ لِمِثْلِ هذا فَلْيَعْمَلِ الْعامِلُونَ."

I say: The hadith of the slaughtering of Death in the form of a ram on the Day of Resurrection is among the famous ones, narrated by both the Shi‘a and the Sunnis; it is the manifestation (تمثل) of eternity on that day.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: شَجَرَةُ الزَّقُّومِ, it is narrated that when the Quraysh heard this verse, they said: "We do not know this tree." Ibn al-Ziba‘ra said: "Zaqqum in the language of the Berbers is dates and butter." In a narration, it was in the dialect of Yemen. So Abu Jahl said to his maidservant: "O maidservant, zaqqimina (feed us Zaqqum)!" So the maidservant brought him dates and butter. He said to his companions: "Eat this zaqqum with which Muhammad threatens you! He claims that the Fire grows trees, while fire burns trees." So Allah, the Glorified, revealed: إِنَّ جَعَلْناها فِتْنَةً لِلظَّالِمِينَ.

I say: This meaning is narrated through numerous paths.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ضَلَّ قَبْلَهُمْ أَكْثَرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٧١)
Most of the former peoples went astray before them, (71)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِم مُّنذِرِينَ (٧٢)
and We had certainly sent warners among them. (72)
فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (٧٣)
So observe how was the fate of those who were warned (73)
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٧٤)
—[all] except Allah’s exclusive servants! (74)
وَلَقَدْ نَادَىٰنَا نُوحٌ فَلَنِعْمَ ٱلْمُجِيبُونَ (٧٥)
Certainly Noah called out to Us, and how well did We respond! (75)
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٦)
We delivered him and his family from their great distress, (76)
وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥ هُمُ ٱلْبَاقِينَ (٧٧)
and made his descendants the survivors, (77)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٧٨)
and left for him a good name among posterity: (78)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧٩)
‘Peace to Noah, throughout the nations!’ (79)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٠)
Thus do We reward the virtuous. (80)
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨١)
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (81)
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (٨٢)
Then We drowned the rest. (82)
۞ وَإِنَّ مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ لَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٨٣)
Indeed Abraham was among his followers, (83)
إِذْ جَآءَ رَبَّهُۥ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (٨٤)
when he came to his Lord with a sound heart [untainted by sin]. (84)
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَاذَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٨٥)
When he said to his father and his people, ‘What is it that you are worshiping? (85)
أَئِفْكًا ءَالِهَةً دُونَ ٱللَّهِ تُرِيدُونَ (٨٦)
Is it a lie, gods other than Allah, that you desire? (86)
فَمَا ظَنُّكُم بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٧)
Then what is your idea about the Lord of all the worlds?’ (87)
فَنَظَرَ نَظْرَةً فِى ٱلنُّجُومِ (٨٨)
Then he made an observation of the stars (88)
فَقَالَ إِنِّى سَقِيمٌ (٨٩)
and said, ‘Indeed I am sick!’ (89)
فَتَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ مُدْبِرِينَ (٩٠)
So they went away leaving him behind. (90)
فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِهِمْ فَقَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٩١)
Then he stole away to their gods and said, ‘Will you not eat? (91)
مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنطِقُونَ (٩٢)
Why do you not speak?’ (92)
فَرَاغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْبًۢا بِٱلْيَمِينِ (٩٣)
Then he attacked them, striking forcefully. (93)
فَأَقْبَلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يَزِفُّونَ (٩٤)
They came running towards him. (94)
قَالَ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مَا تَنْحِتُونَ (٩٥)
He said, ‘Do you worship what you have yourselves carved, (95)
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٦)
when Allah has created you and whatever you make?’ (96)
قَالُوا۟ ٱبْنُوا۟ لَهُۥ بُنْيَـٰنًا فَأَلْقُوهُ فِى ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٩٧)
They said, ‘Build a structure for him and cast him into a huge fire.’ (97)
فَأَرَادُوا۟ بِهِۦ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْأَسْفَلِينَ (٩٨)
So they sought to outwit him, but We made them the lowermost. (98)
وَقَالَ إِنِّى ذَاهِبٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى سَيَهْدِينِ (٩٩)
He said, ‘Indeed I am going toward my Lord, who will guide me.’ (99)
رَبِّ هَبْ لِى مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٠٠)
‘My Lord! Give me [an heir], one of the righteous.’ (100)
فَبَشَّرْنَـٰهُ بِغُلَـٰمٍ حَلِيمٍ (١٠١)
So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son. (101)
فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ ٱلسَّعْىَ قَالَ يَـٰبُنَىَّ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ فِى ٱلْمَنَامِ أَنِّىٓ أَذْبَحُكَ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا تَرَىٰ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ ٱفْعَلْ مَا تُؤْمَرُ ۖ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٠٢)
When he was old enough to assist in his endeavour, he said, ‘My son! I see in dreams that I am sacrificing you. See what you think.’ He said, ‘Father! Do whatever you have been commanded. If Allah wishes, you will find me to be patient.’ (102)
فَلَمَّآ أَسْلَمَا وَتَلَّهُۥ لِلْجَبِينِ (١٠٣)
So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s will], and he had laid him down on his forehead, (103)
وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ أَن يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ (١٠٤)
We called out to him, ‘O Abraham! (104)
قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ ٱلرُّءْيَآ ۚ إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٠٥)
You have indeed fulfilled your vision! Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous! (105)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْبَلَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٠٦)
This was indeed a manifest test.’ (106)
وَفَدَيْنَـٰهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٠٧)
Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice, (107)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٠٨)
and left for him a good name in posterity: (108)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (١٠٩)
‘Peace be to Abraham!’ (109)
كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١١٠)
Thus do We reward the virtuous. (110)
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١١)
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (111)
وَبَشَّرْنَـٰهُ بِإِسْحَـٰقَ نَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١١٢)
And We gave him the good news of [the birth of] Isaac, a prophet, one of the righteous. (112)
وَبَـٰرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَعَلَىٰٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا مُحْسِنٌ وَظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ مُبِينٌ (١١٣)
And We blessed him and Isaac. Among their descendants [some] are virtuous, and [some] who manifestly wrong themselves. (113)

(Commentary)

This is a follow-up intended for the previous context, which addressed their polytheism (الشرك), their denial of Allah’s signs (آيات الله), and the threat of painful punishment against them.
