It states that most of the former peoples went astray just as they have gone astray, and denied the warning messengers just as they have denied them. It cites as evidence the stories of Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Harun, Ilyas, Lut, and Yunus (peace be upon them). The verses quoted contain an allusion to the story of Nuh and a summary of the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ ضَلَّ قَبْلَهُمْ أَكْثَرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ (Most of the ancients had gone astray before them) until His saying: الْمُخْلَصِينَ (the devoted ones). This speech is directed toward warning the polytheists of this nation by drawing a parallel between them and the perished nations before them. Indeed, most of those ancients went astray just as these have gone astray, and messengers were sent to them as warners (منذرون) just as a warner was sent to these people. They denied them, and the consequence of their affair was destruction, except for the devoted ones (المخلصين) among them.

The lam in لَقَدْ ضَلَ is for an oath (القسم), as it is in لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا. The first instance of المنذرين (the warners) is with a kasrah on the dhal, referring to the messengers; the second is with a fathah on the dhal, المنذرين (the warned), referring to the ancient nations. As for إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ (except the servants of Allah), if the intended meaning is those among the nations who were devoted (المخلصين), then it is a connected exception (استثناء متصل). If it encompasses the prophets, it is a disconnected exception (منقطع), unless the non-prophets are given preponderance over them. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ نادانا نُوحٌ فَلَنِعْمَ الْمُجِيبُونَ (Certainly Noah called out to Us, and how excellent responders were We!). Both lams are for an oath, which indicates the utmost care (العناية) given to Nuh’s call and the response of the Exalted. The Exalted has praised Himself in His response, for the implied meaning is: "How excellent responders were We." The plural form of "responders" (المجيبون) is used for magnification (التعظيم). Nuh’s call—as indicated by the context—was his invocation against his people and his seeking of aid from his Lord, as recounted in His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ نُوحٌ رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً (And Noah said, ‘My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless’) (Nuh: 26), and in His saying, the Exalted: فَدَعا رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ (So he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘I am overcome, so help [me]!’) (Al-Qamar: 10).

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَجَّيْناهُ وَأَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ (And We delivered him and his family from the great distress). Distress (الكرب) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is intense grief (الغم الشديد). What is meant by it here is the Flood or the harm from his people. By "his family" (أهله), it means the members of his household and the believers among his people. The Exalted said in Surah Hud: قُلْنَا احْمِلْ فِيها مِنْ كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ وَمَنْ آمَنَ (We said, ‘Carry in it a pair of every kind [of animal], and your family, except those against whom the edict has already been passed, and those who have faith’) (Hud: 40). The term "family" (الأهل), just as it is applied to a man's wife and sons, is applied to everyone who is among his close associates.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْباقِينَ (And We made his descendants the survivors). That is, the survivors among mankind after their generation. We have discussed this meaning in the story of Nuh in Surah Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ (And We left for him [a good name] among posterity). What is meant by "leaving" (الترك) is preservation (الإبقاء), and by "posterity" (الآخرين) is the subsequent nations other than the first ones. This sentence is also mentioned after the mention of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in this Surah. In the same story in Surah al-Shu‘ara, it is substituted by his saying: وَاجْعَلْ لِي لِسانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ (And appoint for me a truthful mention among posterity) (Al-Shu‘ara: 84). We derived from there that the meaning of "truthful mention" (لسان صدق) is that Allah should raise after him those who would uphold his call and invite to his creed, which is the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد).

This supports the view that what is meant by "preservation among posterity" is that the Exalted kept alive the call of Nuh (peace be upon him) to monotheism (التوحيد) and his struggle in the way of Allah, era after era and generation after generation, until the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: سَلامٌ عَلى نُوحٍ فِي الْعالَمِينَ (Peace be to Noah among all the worlds!). What is meant by "all the worlds" (العالمين) is all of them, as it is a plural defined by the article al-, signifying generality (العموم). It appears that what is meant is the worlds of human beings, their nations, and their groups until the Day of Resurrection. For it is a blessed and pure greeting from Allah, gifted to him by the human nations whenever any good occurs among them, whether in belief or action. This is because he (peace be upon him) was the first to rise for the call of monotheism (التوحيد) and the refutation of polytheism (الشرك) and its attendant deeds. He endured the severest trials in that for nearly a thousand years, a feat in which no one shared with him. Thus, he has a share in every good that occurs among them until the Day of Resurrection. There is no greeting of such breadth found in the speech of the Exalted for anyone below him.

It has also been said: What is meant by "all the worlds" (العالمين) are the worlds of the angels and the two heavy ones (الثقلين)—the jinn and mankind.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ (Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous). This is a justification for the honor bestowed upon him, such as answering his call, delivering him and his family from the great distress, preserving his descendants, leaving for him a mention among posterity, and the peace upon him among all the worlds. The comparison of his reward to the reward of the virtuous (المحسنين) in general is in terms of the principle of a good reward, not in its specific details. Thus, it does not necessitate that everyone shares in what was exclusive to him (peace be upon him), and this is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (He was indeed one of Our faithful servants). This is a justification for his virtue (إحسانه) indicated by the previous sentence. This is because he (peace be upon him), by virtue of being a servant (عبداً) of Allah in the true sense of the word, would neither desire nor do anything except what Allah willed. And by virtue of being among the true believers (المؤمنين), he would not hold any belief except the truth. This permeated all the pillars of his being. Whoever is like that, nothing issues from him except what is good and beautiful; thus, he was among the virtuous (المحسنين).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ (Then We drowned the others). The "then" (ثم) indicates a sequence in the discourse (تراخي كلامي) rather than a temporal sequence (زماني). By "the others" (الآخرين), it means his polytheistic people.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ لَإِبْراهِيمَ (Indeed Abraham was among his followers). The followers (الشيعة) — are the people who follow others and walk in their footsteps. In short, anyone who agrees with another in his way (طريقته) is among his followers (شيعته), whether he preceded or followed him. The Exalted said: وَحِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ ما يَشْتَهُونَ كَما فُعِلَ بِأَشْياعِهِمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ (A barrier will be set between them and what they desire, as was done with their likes before) (Saba: 54).

The apparent context is that the pronoun in "his followers" (شيعته) refers to Nuh; meaning that Ibrahim was among those who agreed with him in his religion, which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد). It has been said that the pronoun refers to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but there is no evidence for this from the wording.

It has been said: Among the beautiful transitions in the arrangement of the verses is following the story of Nuh (peace be upon him)—who is the second Adam and the father of mankind—with the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), who is the father of the prophets. To him trace the lineages of most prophets after him, and upon his religion rely the living monotheistic religions today, such as the religions of Musa, ‘Isa, and Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Furthermore, Nuh (peace be upon him) was delivered by Allah from drowning, and Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was delivered by Allah from burning.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ جاءَ رَبَّهُ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (When he came to his Lord with a sound heart). His coming to his Lord is a metaphor for his affirmation of Him and his faith in Him. This is supported by the fact that what is meant by the soundness of the heart (سلامة القلب) is its being devoid of everything that harms affirmation and faith in Allah, the Glorified—such as manifest and hidden polytheism (الشرك), evil character traits, the effects of sins, and any attachment to other than Him that might attract a person and disturb the purity of his orientation toward Him, the Glorified.

From this, it becomes clear that what is meant by the sound heart (القلب السليم) is that which has no attachment to other than the Exalted, as mentioned in the hadith, and it will come, Allah willing, in the upcoming discussion of narrations.

It has been said: What is meant by it is a heart sound from polytheism (الشرك). This can be interpreted in a way that returns to the first meaning. It has also been said: What is meant by it is a sorrowful heart, but this is as you see [unlikely].

The adverbial phrase (الظرف) in the verse is connected to His previous saying: مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ (among his followers). In adverbial phrases, that which is not permitted elsewhere is tolerated. It has also been said that it is connected to an implied "Remember" (اذكر).

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ ما ذا تَعْبُدُونَ (When he said to his father and his people, ‘What is it that you worship?’). That is, what thing do you worship? He only asked them about their object of worship while seeing that they worshiped idols out of wonder and astonishment.

His saying, the Exalted: أَإِفْكاً آلِهَةً دُونَ اللهِ تُرِيدُونَ (Is it a lie—gods besides Allah—that you desire?). That is, do you intend gods besides Allah as a lie (إفكاً) and a fabrication (افتراء)? He placed "lie" and "gods" first because his concern was focused on that.

His saying, the Exalted: فَنَظَرَ نَظْرَةً فِي النُّجُومِ فَقالَ إِنِّي سَقِيمٌ (Then he took a look at the stars, and said, ‘I am indeed sick!’). There is no doubt that the apparent meaning of the two verses is that his (peace be upon him) informing them that he was sick was linked to his look at the stars and based upon it. His look at the stars was either to determine the hour and the specific time—like one who has a periodic fever and determines its time by the rising of a star, its setting, or a specific position of the stars—or to gain knowledge of future events, which the astrologers believed the celestial positions indicated. The Sabians were excessive in this, and there was a large number of them in his (peace be upon him) time.

According to the first view: When the people of the city wanted to go out altogether for a festival of theirs, he looked at the stars and informed them that he was sick, that the ailment would soon overcome him, and thus he would not be able to go out with them.

According to the second view: He (peace be upon him) looked at the stars at that time with the look of astrologers and informed them that they indicated he would become sick, so it was not within his power to go out with them.

The first of the two views is more appropriate to his (peace be upon him) state, as he was in such a state of sincere monotheism (إخلاص التوحيد) that he did not see any influence for other than the Exalted. We have no strong evidence indicating that he (peace be upon him) was not sick at all during those days. The Quran has reported his informing that he was sick, and the Glorified mentioned shortly before that he came to his Lord with a sound heart (بقلب سليم); therefore, lying or vain speech is not permissible for him.

Others have different views on the two verses, the most plausible of which is that his look at the stars and his informing of sickness were among the double-entendres (المعاريض) in speech. Double-entendres (المعاريض) — are when a man says something by which he intends one thing, while the listener understands something else. Perhaps he (peace be upon him) looked at the stars with the look of a monotheist at the handiwork of the Exalted, seeking evidence for the Exalted and His oneness, while they thought he was looking at them with the look of an astrologer to seek evidence for events. Then he said: "I am sick," meaning that sickness would eventually overcome him, for a human being is never free from some sickness or disease in his life, as he said: وَإِذا مَرِضْتُ فَهو يَشْفِينِ (and when I get sick, He heals me) (Al-Shu‘ara: 80). They, however, thought he was informing them of his sickness on the day they were to go out for their festival. The factor that made this preferable to him was his concern with turning (الرواغ) toward their idols and breaking them.

However, this view is based on the assumption that he was healthy and not sick on that day, and you have heard that there is no evidence for this. Furthermore, double-entendres are not permissible for prophets because they would remove trust in their speech.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ مُدْبِرِينَ (So they left him, turning their backs). The plural pronoun refers to the people, and the singular pronoun refers to Ibrahim (peace be upon him). That is, they went out of the city and left him behind.

His saying, the Exalted: فَراغَ إِلى آلِهَتِهِمْ فَقالَ أَلا تَأْكُلُونَ ما لَكُمْ لا تَنْطِقُونَ (Then he turned to their gods and said, ‘Will you not eat? What is the matter with you that you do not speak?’). Turning (الروغ والرواغ والروغان) — is to deviate and incline. It is said its origin is to incline to one side to deceive the one who intends him.

In his saying: أَلا تَأْكُلُونَ (Will you not eat?), there is support for what was mentioned that the polytheists used to place food near their gods on their festival days.

His saying: أَلا تَأْكُلُونَ؟ ما لَكُمْ لا تَنْطِقُونَ (Will you not eat? What is the matter with you that you do not speak?) is his addressing their gods while they were inanimate objects. He knew they were inanimate, neither eating nor speaking, but his intense emotion and rage led him to represent their position as that of rational beings, and then to hold them accountable as rational beings are held accountable, just as is done with criminals.

So he looked at them, and they had bodies in the form of those who nourish themselves and eat, and there was some food near them. He was filled with rage and surged with emotion, so he said: "Will you not eat?" He heard no answer from them, so he said: "What is the matter with you that you do not speak?" while you are gods whom your worshipers claim are rational, powerful, and managers of their affairs. When he heard no sound from them, he turned upon them, striking them with the right hand.

His saying, the Exalted: فَراغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْباً بِالْيَمِينِ (Then he turned upon them, striking [them] with the right hand). That is, it followed from that address that he leaned over their gods, striking them with the right hand, or with strength (بِقوة), based on the view that "the right hand" (اليمين) means strength.

The saying of some that "the right hand" (اليمين) means the oath (القسم), and the meaning is that he leaned over them striking because of the oath he had previously made—which was his saying: تَاللهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنامَكُمْ (By Allah, I will surely devise a stratagem against your idols) (Al-Anbiya: 57)—is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: فأَقْبَلُوا إِلَيْهِ يَزِفُّونَ (They came running toward him). Running (الزف والزفيف) — is to hasten in walking. That is, they came to Ibrahim while they were hastening out of concern for the incident which they suspected he had caused.

In the speech, there is brevity (إيجاز) and omission (حذف) regarding the news of their return to the city, their discovery of what had been done to the idols, their investigation of the matter, and their suspicion of him (peace be upon him), which is mentioned in Surah al-Anbiya.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَتَعْبُدُونَ ما تَنْحِتُونَ وَاللهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَما تَعْمَلُونَ (He said, ‘Do you worship what you [yourselves] carve, while Allah has created you and what you make?’). In this, there is brevity and omission regarding the account of his being seized, brought before the eyes of the people, his questioning, and other matters.

The interrogation is for rebuke (التوبيخ), and it also contains an argument for the invalidity of their way. He is saying: That which a human carves with his own hand is not fit to be a lord for the human or an object of worship for him, while Allah, the Glorified, created the human and what he makes. Creation is inseparable from management (التدبير); thus, He is the Lord of the human, and it is foolishness to leave this and worship that.

From this, it has become clear that the most apparent view is that the ما in His saying ما تَنْحِتُونَ (what you carve) is a relative pronoun (موصولة), and the implied meaning is "that which you carve." Likewise in His saying وَما تَعْمَلُونَ (and what you make). Some have allowed that ما in it be an infinitive particle (مصدرية), but this is very far-fetched in the first instance.

There is no harm in attributing creation to what the human makes or to his action, because what the human intends and does by way of his choice is intended by Allah, the Glorified, by way of the human's intention and choice. This type of connection of the Will (الإرادة) to the act does not necessitate the invalidity of the influence of the human's will, nor the act's departure from choice and its becoming compelled (مجبراً) upon it; this is evident.

If the intended meaning were the attribution of the creation of their acts to Allah, the Glorified, without a medium—not by way of their will, but by the connection of His Will to the act itself—and it implied compulsion (الجبر), then the statement would be closer to being an excuse for them than a rebuke and condemnation, and the argument would be for them, not against them.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا ابْنُوا لَهُ بُنْياناً فَأَلْقُوهُ فِي الْجَحِيمِ (They said, ‘Build a structure for him and cast him into the furnace’). Structure (البنيان) — is the infinitive of "to build" (بنى يبني), and what is meant by it is the building itself. The furnace (الجحيم) — is the fire in its intense blazing.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَرادُوا بِهِ كَيْداً فَجَعَلْناهُمُ الْأَسْفَلِينَ (They desired to outwit him, but We made them the lowliest). Stratagem (الكيد) — is a ruse (الحيلة), and what is meant is their ruse to destroy him and burn him with fire.

His saying: فَجَعَلْناهُمُ الْأَسْفَلِينَ (but We made them the lowliest) is a metaphor for making Ibrahim above them, such that their stratagem did not affect him at all, as the Glorified said: يا نارُ كُونِي بَرْداً وَسَلاماً عَلى إِبْراهِيمَ (O fire! Be cool and safe for Abraham!) (Al-Anbiya: 69).

With this, a chapter from the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) is concluded: his initial rising against the worship of idols, his disputation with their worshipers, and the conclusion of his affair with his being cast into the fire and Allah’s nullification of their stratagem.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ إِنِّي ذاهِبٌ إِلى رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ (He said, ‘Indeed I am going to my Lord; He will guide me’). This is another chapter from his (peace be upon him) stories, mentioning his resolve to migrate from among his people, his seeking a righteous son from Allah, the response to that, the story of his sacrifice, and the descent of the ransom.

So his saying: وَقالَ إِنِّي ذاهِبٌ إِلى رَبِّي (He said, ‘Indeed I am going to my Lord’) etc., is like the fulfillment of what he had promised them when addressing Azar: وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَأَدْعُوا رَبِّي عَسى أَلَّا أَكُونَ بِدُعاءِ رَبِّي شَقِيًّا (I will keep away from you and what you invoke besides Allah, and I will invoke my Lord. Hopefully, I will not be disappointed in invoking my Lord) (Maryam: 48). From this, it is known that his meaning by "going to his Lord" is going to a place where he could devote himself to His worship and invocation, which is the Holy Land.

The saying of some that the meaning is "I am going to where my Lord commanded me" has no evidence.

Likewise, the saying of some that the meaning is "I am going to meet my Lord when they cast me into the fire, so I shall die and meet my Lord, and He will guide me to Paradise."

In this—as has been said—the end of the verse does not suit it, which is his saying: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ (My Lord! Give me [a child] from among the righteous), as well as His saying after it: فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son).

His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ (My Lord! Give me [a child] from among the righteous). This is a recounting of Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) prayer and his request for a child; that is, he said: "My Lord! Give me..." etc. He conditioned it by his being from among the righteous.

His saying, the Exalted: فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son). That is, We gave him the good news that We would provide him with a forbearing son. In this is an indication that he would be male and would reach the stage of youth (الغلمان). The use of "youth" (الغلومة) in his description, even though he reached the stage of manhood, is to indicate his state in which the quality of his perfection and the purity of his essence would appear, which is his forbearance (حلمه) that enabled him to be patient for the sake of Allah, when he said: يا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ ما تُؤْمَرُ سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ (O my father! Do whatever you are bidden. You will find me, if Allah wills, to be one of the patient).

None of the prophets in the Quran is described with forbearance (الحلم) except this noble prophet in this verse, and his father in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّاهٌ مُنِيبٌ (Abraham was indeed forbearing, tender-hearted, [and] penitent) (Hud: 75).

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ السَّعْيَ قالَ يا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرى فِي الْمَنامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ فَانْظُرْ ما ذا تَرى (When he was old enough to assist him, he said, ‘O my son! I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you. See what you think’) etc. The fa at the beginning of the verse is elided (فصيحة), indicating something omitted. The implied meaning is: "When he was born to him, grew up, and reached the age of assisting him." What is meant by "reaching the age of assisting" (بلوغ السعي) is reaching an age where one normally strives for the needs of life, which is the age of adolescence (سن الرهاق). The meaning is: "When the boy reached adolescence, he said to him: 'O my son...'" etc.

His saying: قالَ يا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرى فِي الْمَنامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ (he said, ‘O my son! I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you’) is Ibrahim’s vision of sacrificing his son. His saying إِنِّي أَرى (I see) indicates the repetition of this vision for him, as in His saying: وَقالَ الْمَلِكُ إِنِّي أَرى (And the king said, ‘I see...’) (Yusuf: 43).

His saying: فَانْظُرْ ما ذا تَرى (See what you think) is from "opinion" (الرأي) in the sense of belief; that is, "Think about what I have said and determine what your opinion is regarding it." This sentence is evidence that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) understood from his dream that it was a command for him to sacrifice, represented to him in the form of the result of the command. Therefore, he sought his son’s opinion on it, testing him to see how he would respond.

His saying: قالَ يا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ ما تُؤْمَرُ سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ (He said, ‘O my father! Do whatever you are bidden. You will find me, if Allah wills, to be one of the patient’) is the son’s response. His saying: يا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ ما تُؤْمَرُ (O my father! Do whatever you are bidden) is an expression of consent to the sacrifice in the form of a command. He said, "Do whatever you are bidden," and did not say, "Sacrifice me," indicating that his father was commanded with an order for which he had nothing but to comply and obey.

His saying: سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ (You will find me, if Allah wills, to be one of the patient) is a comforting of his father’s soul, that he would not be distressed by it nor do anything that would stir the father’s grief over his son covered in his blood. He added purity upon purity in his speech when he conditioned his promise of patience with his saying: إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ (if Allah wills). Thus, he indicated that his being characterized by this noble quality—namely, patience—was not from himself, nor was its rein in his hand; rather, it is from the gifts and favors of Allah. If He wills, he is clothed in it, and He has the right not to will, and thus strip it from him.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا أَسْلَما وَتَلَّهُ لِلْجَبِينِ (So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s liberty], and he had laid him down on his forehead). Submission (الإسلام) — is contentment and surrender. To lay down (التل) — is to throw down. The forehead (الجبين) — is one of the two sides of the brow. The lam in لِلْجَبِينِ is to clarify what the throwing down occurred upon, like His saying: يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ سُجَّداً (they fall down on their faces in prostration) (Al-Isra: 107). The meaning is: "When they both surrendered—Ibrahim and his son—to the command of Allah and were content with it, and Ibrahim threw him down on his forehead."

The response to "when" (لما) is omitted, as an allusion to the severity of the calamity and the bitterness of the event.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنادَيْناهُ أَنْ يا إِبْراهِيمُ قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ الرُّؤْيا (We called out to him, ‘O Abraham! You have indeed fulfilled the vision!’) is conjoined to the omitted response of "when." His saying: قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ الرُّؤْيا (You have indeed fulfilled the vision!) means: "You have brought it to the place of truth, made it true, and complied with the command We ordered you therein." That is, the command therein was a test (امتحانياً), for the compliance of which it is sufficient for the one commanded to be prepared for the act and on the verge of it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْبَلاءُ الْمُبِينُ (Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. This was indeed a manifest trial). The indication in "thus" (كذلك) is to the story of the sacrifice insofar as it was a difficult ordeal and a severe trial. The indication in "this" (هذا) is also to it, and it is a justification for the severity of the matter.

The meaning is: "In this manner do We reward the virtuous; We test them with trials that are difficult in appearance but easy in meaning. When they complete the trial, We reward them with the best reward in this world and the Hereafter. That is because what We tried Ibrahim with was indeed the manifest trial (البلاء المبين)."

His saying, the Exalted: وَفَدَيْناهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ (Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice). That is, We ransomed his son with a great sacrifice. It was a ram brought by Jibra’il from Allah, the Glorified, as a ransom, according to the reports. What is meant by the greatness of the sacrifice is the greatness of its status by its being from Allah, the Glorified, and it is that with which He ransomed the one to be sacrificed (الذبيح).

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ (And We left for him [a good name] among posterity). The discussion on this has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: سَلامٌ عَلى إِبْراهِيمَ (Peace be to Abraham!). A greeting from the Exalted upon him. In the indefiniteness of "peace" (سلام), there is magnification (تفخيم) for it.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ إِنَّه مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. He was indeed one of Our faithful servants). The interpretation of these two verses has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبَشَّرْناهُ بِإِسْحاقَ نَبِيًّا مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ (And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous). The pronoun refers to Ibrahim (peace be upon him).

Know that this verse, containing the good news of Ishaq, by its occurrence after the previous good news in His saying: فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son), followed by His saying: فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ السَّعْيَ (When he was old enough to assist him) until the end of the story, is apparent—almost explicit—or rather explicit, that the one to be sacrificed (الذبيح) was other than Ishaq, and he was Isma‘il (peace be upon him). We have detailed the discussion on this in the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in Surah al-An‘am.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبارَكْنا عَلَيْهِ وَعَلى إِسْحاقَ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِما مُحْسِنٌ وَظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ مُبِينٌ (And We blessed him and Isaac, and among the descendants of the two there is the virtuous, and [also] those who are manifest wrongdoers to themselves). Blessing (المباركة) — upon something is to place goodness, growth, and stability in it. That is, We placed in what We gave Ibrahim and Ishaq stable goodness and growth.

It is possible that His saying: وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِما (and among the descendants of the two) etc., is a contextual clue that what is meant by His saying: بارَكْنا (We blessed) is the giving of blessing and abundance in his children and the children of Ishaq. The rest is clear.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In the Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (with a sound heart), he said: "The sound heart is that which meets Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, while there is no one in it besides Him."

And in it, he said: "The heart sound from doubt."

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Hujr, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'He criticized their gods, then took a look at the stars and said: "I am indeed sick." Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: "By Allah, he was not sick, and he did not lie."'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and in some of them: "Ibrahim was not sick and he did not lie—he only meant sick in his religion, seeking [the truth]."

The narrations regarding the story of Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) disputation with his people, his breaking of the idols, and his being cast into the fire have already preceded in the interpretation of Surahs al-An‘am, Maryam, al-Anbiya, and al-Shu‘ara.

In al-Tawhid, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a hadith: A man asked him about verses that were ambiguous to him. He said: "I have informed you that for many a thing in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, its interpretation (تأويله) is other than its literal revelation (تنزيله), and it does not resemble the speech of humans—and I shall inform you of a portion of it so that you may be satisfied, if Allah wills."

"Among that is the saying of Ibrahim (peace be upon him): إِنِّي ذاهِبٌ إِلى رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ (Indeed I am going to my Lord; He will guide me). His going to his Lord was his turning toward Him in worship, exertion, and proximity to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. Do you not see that its interpretation is other than its literal revelation?"

In it, with its chain of narration from al-Fath ibn Yazid al-Jurjani, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him), he said: "O Fath, indeed for Allah there are two wills (إرادتين) and two volitions (مشيئتين): a will of decree (إرادة حتم) and a will of resolve (إرادة عزم). He forbids while He wills that, and He commands while He does not will—have you not seen that He forbade Adam and his wife from eating from the tree while He willed that? Had He not willed it, they would not have eaten; and had they eaten, their desire would have overcome the Will of Allah. And He commanded Ibrahim to sacrifice his son Isma‘il (peace be upon him) and willed that he not sacrifice him—had He not willed that he not sacrifice him, Ibrahim’s will would have overcome the Will of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic." I said: "You have relieved me, may Allah relieve you."

And from Amali al-Shaykh, with its chain of narration to Sulayman ibn Yazid, who said: "‘Ali ibn Musa narrated to us, saying: 'My father narrated to me from his father, from Abu Ja‘far, from his father, from his fathers (peace be upon them), who said: "The one to be sacrificed (الذبيح) was Isma‘il (peace be upon him)."'" I say: The like of it is narrated in al-Majma‘ from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them). There are many other narrations with this content from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). It has occurred in some of their narrations that it was Ishaq, but that is discarded due to its contradiction with the Book.

And from al-Faqih: al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the one to be sacrificed (الذبيح), who was he? He said: "Isma‘il, because Allah, the Exalted, mentioned his story in His Book—then He said: وَبَشَّرْناهُ بِإِسْحاقَ نَبِيًّا مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ (And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous)."

I say: This is what preceded in the commentary on the verse, that the verse by its context is apparent, rather explicit, in that.

In al-Majma‘, from Ibn Ishaq: "Whenever Ibrahim visited Isma‘il and Hajar, he would be carried on the Buraq. He would set out from Syria in the morning, take a midday nap in Mecca, and return from Mecca to spend the night with his family in Syria. When [Isma‘il] was old enough to assist him, he saw in a dream that he was to sacrifice him. He said to him: 'O my son! Take the rope and the large knife (المدية), then come with us to this mountain pass to gather wood.'"

"When Ibrahim was alone with his son in the mountain pass of Thabir, he informed him of what Allah had mentioned regarding him. He said: 'O my father! Tighten my bonds so that I do not struggle, and keep your clothes away from me so that none of my blood splashes on them—lest my mother see it—and sharpen your blade, and pass the knife quickly over my throat so that it may be easier for me, for death is severe.' Ibrahim said to him: 'What an excellent helper you are, O my son, in the matter of Allah!'"

Then he recounted the story, in which: "Then he bent over him with the knife—and Jibra’il turned the knife onto its back—and he pulled the ram from the direction of Thabir and pulled the boy from under it, and placed the ram in the place of the boy. A call came from the left side of the Khayf Mosque: 'O Ibrahim! You have indeed fulfilled the vision!'"

I say: The narrations regarding the story are many and are not free from variation.

In it: al-‘Ayyashi narrated with his chain of narration from Burayd ibn Mu‘awiyah al-‘Ijli, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'How much time was there between the good news to Ibrahim of Isma‘il and the good news of Ishaq (peace be upon them)?' He said: 'There were five years between the two pieces of good news. Allah, the Glorified, said: فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son), meaning Isma‘il—and it was the first good news with which Allah gave Ibrahim (peace be upon him) tidings of a child.'"

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (١١٤)
Certainly We favoured Moses and Aaron, (114)
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُمَا وَقَوْمَهُمَا مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١١٥)
and delivered them and their people from their great distress, (115)
وَنَصَرْنَـٰهُمْ فَكَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (١١٦)
and We helped them so that they became the victors. (116)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلْمُسْتَبِينَ (١١٧)
We gave them the illuminating scripture (117)
وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (١١٨)
and guided them to the straight path, (118)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِمَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١١٩)
and left for them a good name in posterity. (119)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (١٢٠)
‘Peace be to Moses and Aaron!’ (120)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٢١)
Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. (121)
إِنَّهُمَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٢٢)
They are indeed among Our faithful servants. (122)
وَإِنَّ إِلْيَاسَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٢٣)
Indeed Ilyas was one of the apostles. (123)
إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٢٤)
When he said to his people, ‘Will you not be Godwary? (124)
أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلًا وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ (١٢٥)
Do you invoke Baal and abandon the best of creators, (125)
ٱللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٢٦)
Allah, your Lord and Lord of your forefathers?,’ (126)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ (١٢٧)
they impugned him. So they will indeed be mustered [in hell] (127)
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (١٢٨)
—[all] except Allah’s exclusive servants. (128)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٢٩)
We left for him a good name in posterity. (129)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِلْ يَاسِينَ (١٣٠)
‘Peace be to Ilyas!’ (130)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣١)
Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. (131)
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٢)
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (132)

(Commentary)

This is a summary of the story of Moses (موسى) and Aaron (هارون), followed by an allusion to the story of Elijah (إلياس), peace be upon him (عليه السلام). It details the blessings Allah bestowed upon them and the punishment of those who denied them, noting that the aspect of mercy therein outweighs the aspect of punishment, and the glad tidings exceed the warnings.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلى مُوسى وَهارُونَ "Certainly We certainly favored Moses and Aaron" (37:114). Favor (المن) refers to the bestowing of blessings (الإنعام). It is possible that what is intended here is the collective blessings He is about to enumerate—which He bestowed upon them and their people—such as deliverance, victory, the granting of the Book, guidance, and others. In this case, His saying: وَنَجَّيْناهُما "And We delivered them..." would be an explanatory apposition (عطف التفسير).

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَجَّيْناهُما وَقَوْمَهُما مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ "And We delivered them and their people from the great agony" (37:115). This refers to the intense distress (الغم الشديد) resulting from Pharaoh’s oppression of them, as he inflicted upon them a grievous punishment, slaughtering their sons and sparing their women.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَصَرْناهُمْ فَكانُوا هُمُ الْغالِبِينَ "And We helped them, so they were the victors" (37:116). This assistance led to their departure from Egypt, their crossing of the sea, and the destruction of Pharaoh and his hosts.

By this, the misconception is dispelled that the apparent context should have mentioned "victory" before "deliverance," since the latter depends on the former. This is because victory (النصر) occurs when the one being helped possesses some degree of power, yet it is insufficient to repel evil until it is completed by divine aid. When the Children of Israel departed Egypt, they possessed some measure of strength; thus, it was appropriate to apply the term "victory" to the assistance given to them in that regard. This contrasts with the fundamental act of their liberation from Pharaoh’s hand, for they were captives and enslaved, possessing no power of their own. In this respect, it was only appropriate to mention "deliverance" (التنجية) rather than "victory."

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْناهُمَا الْكِتابَ الْمُسْتَبِينَ "And We gave them the manifest Book" (37:117). That is, it clarifies hidden unknowns and elucidates them; these are the matters people require for their worldly life and their hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهَدَيْناهُمَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ "And We guided them to the straight path" (37:118). What is intended here is guidance (الهداية) in the full sense of the word. For this reason, He specified the two of them with it and did not include their people alongside them. We have previously discussed the meaning of guidance to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — in Surah al-Fatihah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِما فِي الْآخِرِينَ "And We left for them [a good name] among posterity" (37:119) until His saying: الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "...the faithful" (37:122). The commentary on these verses has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ إِلْياسَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ "Indeed Elijah was one of the apostles" (37:123). It is said that he (عليه السلام) was from the lineage of Aaron and was sent to Baalbek; however, the author did not mention anything in his discourse to substantiate this.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَلا تَتَّقُونَ أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلاً وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ الْخالِقِينَ "When he said to his people, ‘Will you not be God-wary? Do you invoke Baal and abandon the Best of creators—" (37:124-125) until His saying: الْأَوَّلِينَ "...the forefathers?" (37:126). This is a portion of his (عليه السلام) mission, in which he calls his people to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and rebukes them for worshipping Baal—an idol they possessed—and abandoning the worship of Allah, the Glorified.

His speech (عليه السلام), despite the rebuke and blame it contains, comprises a complete proof for His Oneness, the Exalted. For his saying: وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ الْخالِقِينَ اللهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ "and abandon the Best of creators, Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers?" (37:125-126) first rebukes them for abandoning the worship of the Best of creators. Creation (الخلق) and origination (الإيجاد), just as they relate to the essences of things, also relate to the system governing them, which is termed "governance" (التدبير). Thus, just as creation belongs to Him, the Exalted, governance also belongs to Him; He is the Governor (المدبر) just as He is the Creator (الخالق). He alluded to this by His saying: اللهَ رَبَّكُمْ "Allah, your Lord" after describing Him, the Exalted, as the Best of creators.

Then he pointed out that His Lordship (الربوبية), the Exalted, is not exclusive to one people over another—unlike idols, of which each people adopts some to the exclusion of others, such that an idol might be a lord for one people but not for others. Rather, He, the Exalted, is the Lord for them and for their forefathers; He is not exclusive to some over others due to the universality of His creation and governance. To this he alluded by his saying: اللهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ "Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers."

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ "But they impugned him. So they will indeed be arraigned [for punishment]" (37:127). That is, they will be resurrected to be brought (ليحضروا) to the punishment. It has been previously mentioned that the term "arraignment" (الإحضار), when used absolutely, conveys the sense of evil.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ "except the chosen servants of Allah" (37:128). This is evidence that there was a group among his people who were of this description.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ "And We left for him [a good name] among posterity" (37:129) until His saying: الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "...the faithful" (37:132). The discussion regarding similar verses has preceded.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلاً "Do you invoke Baal," he said: "They had an idol they called Baal."

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission to Qadih, from Imam al-Sadiq, Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father, from his forefathers, from Imam Ali (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: سلام على آل يس "Peace be upon the progeny of Yasin." He said: يس محمد صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ونحن آل يس "Yasin is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and we are the progeny of Yasin (آل يس)."

I say: A similar report is narrated in al-Uyun from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام). This interpretation is based on the recitation of آل يس (the progeny of Yasin), as recited by Nafi', Ibn 'Amir, Ya'qub, and Zayd.

(A Discourse on the Story of Elijah (عليه السلام))

1. His Story in the Quran: His name (عليه السلام) is not mentioned in the Noble Quran except in this place and in Surah al-An'am, during the mention of the guidance of the Prophets, where He says: وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيى وَعِيسى وَإِلْياسَ كُلٌّ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ "And Zechariah, John, Jesus and Elijah; each was one of the righteous" (6:85).

The Exalted did not mention anything of his story in this Surah except that he used to call a people who worshipped Baal to the worship of Allah, the Glorified. A group of them believed in him with sincere faith, while others—who were the majority of the people—denied him, and they shall indeed be arraigned.

Allah, the Glorified, praised him in Surah al-An'am with the same praise He gave to the Prophets in general, and He praised him in this Surah as being among His faithful, virtuous servants, and He greeted him with peace, based on the well-known recitation: سَلامٌ عَلى إِلْ ياسِينَ "Peace be upon Ilyasin" (37:130).

2. The Narrations Concerning Him: Various conflicting reports have been narrated regarding him (عليه السلام), as is the case with most reports concerning the stories of the Prophets that recount wonders. For instance, it is narrated from Ibn Mas'ud that Elijah is Idris; and from Ibn Abbas, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), that al-Khidr is Elijah. It is narrated from Wahb, Ka'b al-Ahbar, and others that Elijah is alive and will not die until the first blast of the Trumpet. From Wahb, it is said that Elijah asked Allah to relieve him of his people, so Allah sent him a beast in the form of a horse the color of fire; he leaped upon it and it departed with him, and Allah clothed him in feathers and light, and removed from him the pleasure of food and drink, so he became among the angels. From Ka'b al-Ahbar, it is said that Elijah is the master of the mountains and the wilderness, and that he is the one whom Allah named Dhu al-Nun. From al-Hasan, it is said that Elijah is entrusted with the deserts and al-Khidr is entrusted with the mountains. From Anas, it is said that Elijah met the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) during one of his journeys; they sat and conversed, then a table descended upon them from heaven, they ate and fed me, then he bid him and me farewell, and then I saw him passing over the clouds toward the sky—among other such reports. In some Shi'a reports, it is mentioned that he (عليه السلام) is alive and immortal, but these are weak, and the apparent meaning of the verses of the story does not support this.

In al-Bihar, regarding the story of Elijah (عليه السلام), from Qisas al-Anbiya, with a chain of transmission to al-Saduq, from Wahb ibn Munabbih—and al-Tha'labi narrated it in al-Ara'is from Ibn Ishaq, and the chroniclers of reports have it in more detail—the tradition is very long, and its summary is: After the kingdom of the Children of Israel split and was divided among them, a tribe of them traveled to Baalbek. They had a king from among them who worshipped an idol named Baal and compelled the people to worship it.

He had a profligate wife who had married seven kings before him and had given birth to ninety children, besides grandchildren. The king would appoint her as his deputy when he was absent, and she would judge among the people. He had a wise, believing scribe who had saved three hundred believers from her hand when she intended to kill them. Neighboring the king's palace was a believing man who owned a garden, and the king used to respect his neighborliness and honor him.

During one of the king's absences, the woman killed the believing neighbor and usurped his garden. When the king returned and learned of this, he rebuked her, but she offered excuses and satisfied him. Consequently, Allah, the Exalted, took an oath upon Himself to take vengeance upon them if they did not repent. He sent Elijah (عليه السلام) to them, calling them to the worship of Allah and informing them of Allah's oath. Their anger toward him intensified, and they intended to torture and kill him. He fled from them to the most rugged mountain there and remained in it for seven years, living on the plants of the earth and the fruits of the trees.

Then Allah caused a son of the king, whom he loved dearly, to fall ill. He sought intercession from Baal, but it did not avail him. It was said to him: "He is angry with you because you have not killed Elijah." So he sent a group of his people to deceive him and capture him. Allah sent a fire upon them and burned them. Then he sent another group of powerful men along with his believing scribe. Elijah went with him to protect him from the king's wrath, but Allah, the Glorified, caused the king's son to die, and his grief distracted him from Elijah, who returned safely.

When time passed, Elijah descended from the mountain and hid in the house of the mother of Jonah (يونس) ibn Matta, while Jonah was a nursing infant. After six months, he returned to the mountain. It happened that Jonah died after that, but Allah brought him back to life through the prayer of Elijah after his mother went out seeking him, found him, and pleaded with him.

Then he asked Allah to take vengeance for him against the Children of Israel and to withhold the rain from them. His prayer was answered, and Allah afflicted them with famine, and they suffered for years. They eventually regretted, came to him, repented, and embraced Islam. He prayed to Allah, and He sent rain upon them, quenching their thirst and reviving their lands.

They complained to him about the destruction of their walls and the lack of seeds for grain. It was revealed to him to command them to sow salt, and He caused chickpeas to grow for them; and to sow sand, and He caused millet to grow for them from it.

Then, when Allah removed the distress from them, they broke the covenant and returned to a state more wicked than they were before. Elijah became weary of this and prayed to Allah to relieve him of them. Allah sent him a horse of fire; Elijah leaped upon it, and Allah raised him to heaven, clothing him in feathers and light, so he was with the angels.

Then Allah set an enemy upon the king and his wife, who sought them out, prevailed over them, and killed them. He threw their carcasses into the garden of that believing man whom they had killed and whose garden they had usurped.

By reflecting upon what this narration recounts, you will have no doubt regarding its weakness.

Ayahs

وَإِنَّ لُوطًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٣٣)
Indeed Lot was one of the apostles. (133)
إِذْ نَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٣٤)
When We delivered him and all his family, (134)
إِلَّا عَجُوزًا فِى ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (١٣٥)
excepting an old woman among those who remained behind, (135)
ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (١٣٦)
We destroyed the rest. (136)
وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِم مُّصْبِحِينَ (١٣٧)
Indeed you pass by them at dawn (137)
وَبِٱلَّيْلِ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٣٨)
and at night. Do you not exercise your reason? (138)
وَإِنَّ يُونُسَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٣٩)
Indeed Jonah was one of the apostles. (139)
إِذْ أَبَقَ إِلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (١٤٠)
When he absconded toward the laden ship, (140)
فَسَاهَمَ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُدْحَضِينَ (١٤١)
he drew lots with them and was the one to be condemned [as one to be thrown overboard]. (141)
فَٱلْتَقَمَهُ ٱلْحُوتُ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ (١٤٢)
Then the fish swallowed him while he was blameworthy. (142)
فَلَوْلَآ أَنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَبِّحِينَ (١٤٣)
Had he not been one of those who celebrate Allah’s glory, (143)
لَلَبِثَ فِى بَطْنِهِۦٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (١٤٤)
he would have surely remained in its belly till the day they will be resurrected. (144)
۞ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُ بِٱلْعَرَآءِ وَهُوَ سَقِيمٌ (١٤٥)
Then We cast him on a bare shore, and he was sick. (145)
وَأَنۢبَتْنَا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِّن يَقْطِينٍ (١٤٦)
So We made a gourd plant grow above him. (146)
وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ مِا۟ئَةِ أَلْفٍ أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ (١٤٧)
We sent him to a [community of] hundred thousand or more, (147)
فَـَٔامَنُوا۟ فَمَتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (١٤٨)
and they believed [in him]. So We provided for them for a while. (148)

(Commentary)

This is a summary of the story of Lot (عليه السلام), followed by the story of Jonah (عليه السلام) and the trial Allah the Exalted put him through with the whale, after he was taken to task for turning away from his people when the punishment was averted from them after it had descended and loomed over them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ لُوطاً لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِذْ نَجَّيْناهُ وَأَهْلَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ — "Indeed Lot was one of the apostles. When We delivered him and his family, all together" (37:133-134). He only delivered him and his family from the punishment that descended upon his people, which was the swallowing up of the earth and the raining down of stones of baked clay, as Allah the Exalted has mentioned elsewhere in His speech.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا عَجُوزاً فِي الْغابِرِينَ — "except an old woman [who remained] among those who stayed behind" (37:135). Meaning, among those who remained in the punishment and were destroyed by it; she was the wife of Lot.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا الْآخَرِينَ — "Then We destroyed the rest" (37:136). Destruction (التدمير) is annihilation (الإهلاك), and "the rest" refers to his people to whom he was sent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مُصْبِحِينَ وَبِاللَّيْلِ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ — "Indeed you pass by them at dawn and at night. Do you not exercise your reason?" (37:137-138). For they are on the route from the Hijaz to the Levant (الشام). What is meant by "passing by them" is passing by their ruined dwellings, which are today covered by water, according to what has been said.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ يُونُسَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِذْ أَبَقَ إِلَى الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ — "Indeed Jonah was one of the apostles. When he ran away to the laden ship" (37:139-140). That is, the ship filled with people. Absconding (الإباق) is the flight of a slave from his master.

What is meant by his "absconding to the ship" was his departure from his people, turning away from them. Although he (عليه السلام) did not disobey his Lord in that departure, nor was there a prohibition from his Lord against leaving, his departure at that time resembled the absconding of a slave from the service of his master. Thus, Allah took him to task for it. Some discussion on this has already preceded in the commentary (تفسير) on His saying, the Exalted: وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ — "And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage, thinking that We would not stint him" (21:87).

His saying, the Exalted: فَساهَمَ فَكانَ مِنَ الْمُدْحَضِينَ — "Then he drew lots and was one of the losers" (37:141). Drawing lots (المساهمة) is casting lots (المقارعة), and being "rebutted" (الإدحاض) means being overcome (الغلبة). That is, he cast lots with those on the ship and was among the defeated. A whale had appeared before their ship, and they were forced to throw one of them into the sea so it would swallow him and leave the ship alone. They cast lots, and it fell upon Jonah (عليه السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْتَقَمَهُ الْحُوتُ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ — "Then the fish swallowed him while he was blameful" (37:142). Swallowing (الالتقام) is devouring (الابتلاع). Blameful (مليم) is from alama (ألام), meaning he entered into blame, just as ahrama (أحرم) means one entered the sacred precinct (الحرم); or it means he became deserving of reproach.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَوْ لا أَنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُسَبِّحِينَ لَلَبِثَ فِي بَطْنِهِ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ — "And had he not been one of those who celebrate Allah’s glory, he would have surely remained in its belly till the day they are resurrected" (37:143-144). Counting him among "those who celebrate Allah's glory" (المسبحين)—who are those from whom glorification (التسبيح) is repeated and firmly established until it becomes an attribute for them—indicates his continuous engagement in glorification for a period of time. It is said: meaning from those who glorified before the whale swallowed him. It is also said: rather, while in the belly of the whale. And it is said: meaning he was among the glorifiers both before the whale swallowed him and while in its belly.

That which is recounted of his glorification in His speech, the Exalted, is His saying in Surah al-Anbiya: فَنادى فِي الظُّلُماتِ أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Then he cried out in the darkness, ‘There is no god except You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!’" (21:87). The necessary implication of this is that he was among the glorifiers specifically in the belly of the whale, or both in it and before it. Thus, the possibility that what is meant is his glorification only before being swallowed by the whale is less likely and should not be adopted.

Furthermore, his glorification along with his confession of wrongdoing in his saying: سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!"—as will come—is a glorification of Him, the Exalted, from what his action (1) of leaving his people and going his own way seemed to imply. His saying: فَلَوْ لا أَنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُسَبِّحِينَ — "And had he not been one of those who celebrate Allah’s glory"—etc., indicates that his glorification was the cause that necessitated his deliverance. The implication of this is that he was only tried with what he was tried with so that he might sanctify Him, the Exalted, and thereby be saved from the grief to which his action had led him, into the courtyard of well-being.

(1) Which is that Allah would not have power over him, as He, the Exalted, said: "and he thought that We would not stint him."

By this, it becomes clear that the primary focus in the speech is his glorification in the belly of the whale specifically; thus, the best of the three opinions is the middle one.

It appears that what is meant by his glorification is his calling out in the darkness with his saying: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "There is no god except You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!" He preceded it with the declaration of monotheism (التهليل) so it would be like the clarifying cause for his glorification, as if he were saying: "There is no deity in truth to whom one may turn besides You; so You are transcendent above what my action seemed to imply—that I was absconding from You, turning away from Your servitude, and heading toward another. I was a wrongdoer to myself in my action, so here I am turning to You, disavowing what my action implied of turning from You to another."

This is the meaning of his glorification, and had it not been for this from him, he would never have been saved, as the cause of his deliverance was confined to glorification (التسبيح) and sanctification (التنزيه) in the sense mentioned.

By this, it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: لَلَبِثَ فِي بَطْنِهِ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ — "he would have surely remained in its belly till the day they are resurrected"—is the perpetual duration of his stay in its belly until he is resurrected and comes out of it, like the grave in which a person is buried and remains until he is resurrected and comes out. He, the Exalted, said: مِنْها خَلَقْناكُمْ وَفِيها نُعِيدُكُمْ وَمِنْها نُخْرِجُكُمْ تارَةً أُخْرى — "From it We created you, into it We will return you, and from it We will bring you forth another time" (20:55).

There is no indication in the verse as to whether he (عليه السلام), on the assumption of remaining, would be alive in the belly of the whale until the day they are resurrected, or dead with its belly being his grave while his body and the body of the whale remained as they were, or in some other manner. Thus, there is no room for the disagreement among commentators as to whether he (عليه السلام) would be alive on this assumption or dead with its belly as his grave, or whether "the day they are resurrected" refers to the first blast of the trumpet in which all creatures die, or the second blast, or if the postponement until the Day of Resurrection is a metaphor for the length of the stay.

His saying, the Exalted: فَنَبَذْناهُ بِالْعَراءِ وَهو سَقِيمٌ — "Then We cast him on a barren shore while he was sick" (37:145). Casting (النبذ) is to throw something or toss it. The barren shore (العراء) is a place where there is no cover to provide shade, whether from a roof, a tent, or a tree.

The meaning, as provided by the context, is that he became one of the glorifiers, so We brought him out of the belly of the whale and cast him out of the water onto a land where there was no shade to seek shelter in, while he was sick.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْبَتْنا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِنْ يَقْطِينٍ — "And We caused a gourd plant to grow over him" (37:146). The gourd (اليقطين) is a type of squash; its leaves are wide and round. Allah caused it to grow over him so he could seek shade under its leaves.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَرْسَلْناهُ إِلى مِائَةِ أَلْفٍ أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ — "And We sent him to [a people of] a hundred thousand or more" (37:147). The word "or" (أو) here is used for progression (الترقي) and carries the meaning of "rather" (بل). What is meant by this group is the people of Nineveh.

His saying, the Exalted: فَآمَنُوا فَمَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى حِينٍ — "They believed, so We gave them enjoyment for a while" (37:148). That is, they believed in him, so We did not punish them nor destroy them with the punishment that had loomed over them; rather, We granted them enjoyment of life and survival until their appointed term.

The verse, in its intimation of the removal of punishment from them and their being granted enjoyment, refers to His saying, the Exalted: فَلَوْ لا كانَتْ قَرْيَةٌ آمَنَتْ فَنَفَعَها إِيمانُها إِلَّا قَوْمَ يُونُسَ لَمَّا آمَنُوا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمْ عَذابَ الْخِزْيِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَمَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى حِينٍ — "Why was there no town that believed so that its belief might benefit it, except the people of Jonah? When they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world, and We gave them enjoyment for a while" (10:98).

The context is not devoid of an intimation—rather, an indication—that what is meant by his being sent in His saying: وَأَرْسَلْناهُ — "And We sent him"—is the command for him to go a second time to the people. And by their belief in His saying: فَآمَنُوا — "They believed"—etc., is their belief in his truthfulness and their following him after they had already believed and repented when they saw the punishment.

From here, the weakness of the argument used by some becomes apparent: that his being sent to the people occurred only after his exit from the belly of the whale, and that he was first commanded to go to the people of Nineveh to call them to Allah while they were worshipping idols, but he found the matter too great and left his home to travel the earth, hoping Allah would divert this duty from him. He then took to the sea and Allah tried him with the whale; then, when he was cast on the barren shore, he was commissioned a second time, so he responded, obeyed, and called them, and they answered, so Allah averted a punishment that had been threatening them if they did not believe.

This is because the context, as you have heard, indicates that his being sent was by a second command, and that their belief was a second belief after the initial belief and repentance. Furthermore, their being granted enjoyment for a while was contingent upon their believing in him, not merely upon the removal of the punishment from them. Allah, the Glorified, would not have left them had they not believed in His messenger a second time, just as they had believed in Him and repented to Him initially during his absence. Understand this.

Moreover, His saying, the Exalted: وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذهبَ مُغاضِباً — "And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage" (21:87)—and His saying: وَلا تَكُنْ كَصاحِبِ الْحُوتِ إِذْ نادى وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ — "and do not be like the Companion of the Fish, when he called out as he was choking with grief" (68:48)—do not align with what they mentioned. Likewise, His saying: إِلَّا قَوْمَ يُونُسَ لَمَّا آمَنُوا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمْ عَذابَ الْخِزْيِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا — "except the people of Jonah? When they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world" (10:98)—for "removal" (الكشف) is only used for a punishment that is currently occurring or looming.

(A Discussion on the Story of Jonah (عليه السلام) in Several Sections)

1 — The Noble Quran only addresses certain aspects of his story and the story of his people. In Surah as-Saffat, it addresses his being sent, then his absconding and boarding the ship, the whale swallowing him, then his deliverance, his being sent to the people, and their belief. He, the Exalted, said: وَإِنَّ يُونُسَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِذْ أَبَقَ إِلَى الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ فَساهَمَ فَكانَ مِنَ الْمُدْحَضِينَ فَالْتَقَمَهُ الْحُوتُ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ فَلَوْ لا أَنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُسَبِّحِينَ لَلَبِثَ فِي بَطْنِهِ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ فَنَبَذْناهُ بِالْعَراءِ وَهُوَ سَقِيمٌ وَأَنْبَتْنا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِنْ يَقْطِينٍ وَأَرْسَلْناهُ إِلى مِائَةِ أَلْفٍ أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ فَآمَنُوا فَمَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى حِينٍ — "Indeed Jonah was one of the apostles. When he ran away to the laden ship, then he drew lots and was one of the losers. Then the fish swallowed him while he was blameful. And had he not been one of those who celebrate Allah’s glory, he would have surely remained in its belly till the day they are resurrected. Then We cast him on a barren shore while he was sick, and We caused a gourd plant to grow over him. And We sent him to [a people of] a hundred thousand or more. They believed, so We gave them enjoyment for a while" (37:139-148).

In Surah al-Anbiya, it addresses his glorification in the belly of the whale and his deliverance. He, the Exalted, said: وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ فَنادى فِي الظُّلُماتِ أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَنَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْغَمِّ وَكَذلِكَ نُنْجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage, thinking that We would not stint him. Then he cried out in the darkness, ‘There is no god except You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!’ So We answered his prayer and delivered him from the agony; and thus do We deliver the faithful" (21:87-88).

In Surah Nun, it addresses his calling out while choking with grief, his exit from the whale's belly, and his being chosen. He, the Exalted, said: فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلا تَكُنْ كَصاحِبِ الْحُوتِ إِذْ نادى وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ لَوْ لا أَنْ تَدارَكَهُ نِعْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ لَنُبِذَ بِالْعَراءِ وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ فَاجْتَباهُ رَبُّهُ فَجَعَلَهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, and do not be like the Companion of the Fish, when he called out as he was choking with grief. Had a blessing from his Lord not come to his aid, he would surely have been cast on the barren shore while he was blameworthy. Then his Lord chose him and made him one of the righteous" (68:48-50).

In Surah Yunus, it addresses the belief of his people and the removal of the punishment from them. He, the Exalted, said: فَلَوْ لا كانَتْ قَرْيَةٌ آمَنَتْ فَنَفَعَها إِيمانُها إِلَّا قَوْمَ يُونُسَ لَمَّا آمَنُوا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمْ عَذابَ الْخِزْيِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَمَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى حِينٍ — "Why was there no town that believed so that its belief might benefit it, except the people of Jonah? When they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world, and We gave them enjoyment for a while" (10:98).

The summary of what is gleaned from the verses by joining them together and considering the surrounding context is that Jonah (عليه السلام) was one of the messengers. Allah the Exalted sent him to his people, who were a large multitude exceeding one hundred thousand. He called them, but they did not respond except with denial and rejection, until a punishment he had threatened them with approached them; then he left from among them.

When the punishment loomed over them and they witnessed it with their own eyes, they resolved to believe and repent to Allah, the Glorified. So Allah removed from them the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world.

Then Jonah (عليه السلام) sought news of their condition and found that the punishment had been removed from them—and it seems he did not know of their belief and repentance—so he did not return to them and went his own way in a state of anger and indignation toward them. His outward state was that of one who absconds from his Lord in a rage, thinking He would not have power over him. He boarded the sea in a laden ship.

A great whale appeared before them, and they found no choice but to throw one of them to it to swallow him so the ship might be saved. They cast lots among themselves, and it fell upon Jonah (عليه السلام). So they threw him into the sea, the whale swallowed him, and the ship was saved.

Then Allah, the Glorified, preserved him alive and whole in its belly for days and nights. Jonah (عليه السلام) knew it was a trial with which Allah had tested him as a taking to task for what he had done. He called out in its belly: أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "There is no god except You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!" (21:87).

Allah answered him and commanded the whale to cast him out. It cast him on a barren shore while he was sick. Allah, the Glorified, caused a gourd plant to grow over him so he could seek shade under its leaves. Then, when his condition stabilized, He sent him to his people, and they answered his call and believed in him, so Allah gave them enjoyment for a while.

The narrations (الأخبار) transmitted through the channels of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), despite their abundance, and some narrations from Sunni channels, share common themes regarding the story of Jonah (عليه السلام) in the manner gleaned from the verses, even if they differ in some external details (1).

2 — His story according to the People of the Book: He (عليه السلام) is mentioned by the name Jonah son of Amittai in several places in the Old Testament, as well as in places in the New Testament where his stay in the belly of the whale is alluded to, but his complete story is not mentioned in either.

Al-Alusi narrated in Ruh al-Ma'ani regarding his story according to the People of the Book—and this is supported by the summary (2) of the story in some of their books: that Allah commanded him to go and call the people of Nineveh (3), which was very great at that time, taking about three days to cross. Their evil had become great and their corruption had increased. He found the matter too great and fled to Tarshish (4). He came to Jaffa (5) and found a ship whose people wanted to go to Tarshish, so he paid the fare and boarded the ship. A great wind arose, the waves increased, and the ship was on the verge of sinking.

The sailors were terrified and threw some cargo into the sea to lighten the ship. At that, Jonah went down into the hold of the ship and fell into a deep sleep. The captain approached him and said: "Why are you sleeping? Get up and call upon your God; perhaps He will deliver us from what we are in and not let us perish."

They said to one another: "Come, let us cast lots to find out on whose account this evil has befallen us." They cast lots, and the lot fell on Jonah. They said to him: "Tell us, what have you done? Where did you come from? Where are you going? From what country are you? From what people are you?" He said to them: "I am a servant of the Lord, the God of heaven, the Creator of the land and the sea," and he told them his story. They were greatly afraid and said to him: "Why have you done what you have done?"—reproaching him for that.

Then they said to him: "What shall we do with you now, so that the sea may become calm for us?" He said: "Throw me into the sea and it will become calm, for it is because of me that this great storm has arisen." The men tried hard to return to the shore but could not. So they took Jonah and threw him into the sea to save everyone on the ship. The sea became calm, and Allah commanded a great whale to swallow him. He remained in its belly for three days and three nights. He prayed to his Lord in its belly and sought His help. So the Glorified commanded the whale, and it cast him onto dry land. Then He said to him: "Arise and go to Nineveh and cry out to its people as I commanded you before."

So he (عليه السلام) went and cried out, saying: "Nineveh shall be overthrown after three days." The men of Nineveh believed in Allah, called for a fast, and all put on sackcloth. The news reached the king, so he rose from his throne, took off his robe, put on sackcloth, and sat in ashes. It was proclaimed that no person or animal should taste food or drink. They cried out to Allah the Exalted and turned away from evil and wrongdoing. So Allah had mercy on them and did not send the punishment upon them.

Jonah became grieved and said: "O my God, it was from this that I fled, for I knew that You are the Merciful, the Compassionate, the Patient, the Relenting. O Lord, take my soul, for death is better for me than life." He said: "O Jonah, are you so very grieved by this?" He said: "Yes, O Lord."

Jonah went out and sat opposite the city. He made a shelter for himself there and sat under it to see what would happen in the city. Allah commanded a gourd, and it grew over his head to be a shade for him from his distress. He rejoiced in the gourd with great joy. Then Allah the Exalted commanded a worm, and it struck the gourd so that it withered. Then a scorching wind blew, and the sun shone upon Jonah's head, so the matter became great for him and he longed for death.

The Lord said: "O Jonah, are you so very grieved over the gourd?" He said: "Yes, O Lord, I am very grieved." The Exalted said: "You are grieved over it, yet you did not labor for it nor did you grow it; rather, it came into being in a night and perished in a night. Should I then not have pity on Nineveh, the great city, in which there are more than twelve myriad people who do not know their right hand from their left, and many cattle?" End quote. The points of divergence between this story and what is gleaned from the Noble Quran are apparent, such as the flight from the message and his lack of satisfaction with the removal of the punishment from them despite his knowledge of their belief and repentance.

If you say: Something similar is mentioned in the Noble Quran, such as the attribution of absconding (الإباق) to him in Surah as-Saffat, and likewise his rage and his thinking that Allah would not have power over him as in Surah al-Anbiya.

I say: There is a difference between the two attributions. Their sacred books—meaning the two Testaments—do not refrain from attributing sins, even destructive major sins, to the prophets (عليه السلام). Thus, there is no motive to interpret the sins attributed to him in a way that removes them from being sins. This is in contrast to the Noble Quran, for it sanctifies their presence from the stain of sins, even minor ones. Therefore, what appears in it that might suggest such a thing is interpreted in the best possible way based on this necessitating context. For this reason, we interpreted His saying: إِذْ أَبَقَ — "when he ran away"—and His saying: مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ — "in a rage, thinking that We would not stint him"—as a description of the state and the appearance of his action.

3 — His praise, the Exalted, for him: Allah, the Glorified, praised him as being among the believers (Surah al-Anbiya: 88), and that He chose him (اجتباه). You have already learned that His choosing him is His purifying the servant for Himself specifically. He also made him among the righteous (Surah Nun: 50). In Surah al-An'am, He counted him among those He counted of the prophets, mentioning that He favored them over all the worlds and guided them to a straight path (Surah al-An'am: 87).

(1) For this reason, we do not cite them, as they are in themselves solitary reports (آحاد) that carry no probative force in such a context, and it is impossible to authenticate their details through the verses, as is apparent to whoever reviews them. (2) Dictionary of the Holy Bible. (3) It was a great city among the cities of Assyria on the bank of the Tigris. (4) The name of a city. (5) A city in the Holy Land.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Faqih, Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) said: "No people have ever cast lots and entrusted their affair to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, except that the arrow of truth came out." He also said: "What judgment is more just than the lot—when the affair is entrusted to Allah? Does Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, not say: فَساهَمَ فَكانَ مِنَ الْمُدْحَضِينَ — 'Then he drew lots and was one of the losers'?"

In al-Bihar, from al-Basa'ir with its chain of transmission from Habbah al-Arani, who said: Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said: "Indeed, Allah presented my guardianship (ولاية) to the inhabitants of the heavens and the inhabitants of the earth—whoever acknowledged it, acknowledged it, and whoever denied it, denied it. Jonah denied it, so Allah imprisoned him in the belly of the whale until he acknowledged it."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning. What is meant is the universal divine guardianship (الولاية الكلية الإلهية), of which he (عليه السلام) was the first to open the door among this nation. It is Allah's standing in the place of His servant in the management of his affairs, such that the servant turns to none but Him and desires nothing but what He desires. This is achieved by traversing the path of servitude (العبودية) which ends with the servant being purified by Allah for Himself, such that none other than Him shares in him.

The outward appearance of what Jonah (عليه السلام) did was something Allah, the Exalted, did not approve of, so it was not capable of being attributed to His will. Thus, Allah tried him with what He tried him with so that he might acknowledge his wrongdoing against himself and that He, the Exalted, is transcendent above willing the like of it.
