The trials and tribulations with which the friends of Allah (الأولياء) are tested are part of the divine upbringing (التربية الإلهية) with which He raises them, perfects them, and elevates their ranks, even if some of them are, from another perspective, a taking to task accompanied by reproach. It has been said: "Tribulation is for the sake of guardianship" (البلاء للولاء).

This is supported by what is in al-Ilal, with its chain of transmission from Abu Basir, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): "For what reason did Allah avert the punishment from the people of Jonah—after it had loomed over them—while He did not do that for any other nation?" He said: "Because it was in the knowledge of Allah that He would avert it from them due to their repentance. He only omitted informing Jonah of that because He wanted to free him for His worship in the belly of the whale—so that he might thereby merit His reward and honor."

وَإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (١٧٣)
and indeed Our hosts will be the victors. (173)
فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (١٧٤)
So leave them alone for a while, (174)
وَأَبْصِرْهُمْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ (١٧٥)
and watch them; soon they will see [the truth of the matter]! (175)
أَفَبِعَذَابِنَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (١٧٦)
Do they seek to hasten Our punishment? (176)
فَإِذَا نَزَلَ بِسَاحَتِهِمْ فَسَآءَ صَبَاحُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (١٧٧)
But when it descends in their courtyard it will be a dismal dawn for those who had been warned. (177)
وَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (١٧٨)
So leave them alone for a while, (178)
وَأَبْصِرْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ (١٧٩)
and watch; soon they will see! (179)
سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ ٱلْعِزَّةِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (١٨٠)
Clear is your Lord, the Lord of Might, of whatever they allege [concerning Him]. (180)
وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَى ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٨١)
Peace be to the apostles! (181)
وَٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٨٢)
All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds. (182)

(Commentary)

The Glorified has previously presented that which clarifies that He is the Lord (الرب) — the Sustainer and Master of all creation — who is alone worthy of worship (المعبود), whom sincere servants (عباد مخلصون) like the honored prophets have worshipped, while others have disbelieved in Him; thus, He saved His servants and seized the disbelievers with a painful punishment. Then, in these verses, He addresses what they believe regarding their deities—namely the angels and the jinn—and their claim that the angels are the daughters of Allah and that there is a kinship (نسب) between Him and the jinn.

The Brahmanical and Buddhist idolaters and the Sabians did not claim femininity for all angels, though they did so for some. However, it is narrated from some tribes of the pagan Arabs, such as Juhaynah, Sulaym, Khuza'ah, and Banu Mulayh, that they claimed femininity for all angels. As for the jinn, the claim that their lineage traces back to Him in some manner is narrated from all of them.

In summary, the Exalted points out in these verses the corruption of their claims, then gives glad tidings of victory to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), threatens them with punishment, and concludes the Surah by hallowing Himself, sending peace upon the messengers, and praising Allah, the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَلِرَبِّكَ الْبَناتُ وَلَهُمُ الْبَنُونَ — "Ask them for a pronouncement: Are daughters for your Lord while sons are for them?" (37:149). The Glorified has analyzed their claim that the angels are the daughters of Allah into its necessary implications (اللوازم): that the angels are His children, that they are female, and that He, the Exalted, reserved daughters for Himself while they are reserved for sons. He then refuted these implications one by one. He refuted their claim that He has daughters while they have sons with His saying: فَاسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَلِرَبِّكَ الْبَناتُ وَلَهُمُ الْبَنُونَ. This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) denouncing their claim because of its implication that they are superior to Allah, since they favor sons over daughters, distance themselves from daughters, and bury them alive.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ خَلَقْنَا الْمَلائِكَةَ إِناثاً وَهُمْ شاهِدُونَ — "Did We create the angels female while they were witnesses?" (37:150). The word "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive (منقطعة), meaning: "Nay, did We create the angels female while they were witnesses?"—that is, witnessing their creation? They were not witnesses to their creation, nor do they have the right to claim such a thing. Masculinity and femininity are matters that cannot be established except through some form of sensory perception (الحس). This is a refutation of their claim regarding the femininity of the angels.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّهُمْ مِنْ إِفْكِهِمْ لَيَقُولُونَ وَلَدَ اللهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ — "Look! It is indeed out of their mendacity that they say, 'Allah has begotten,' and they are indeed liars." (37:151-152). This is a refutation of their claim of procreation by stating it is part of their mendacity (الإفك) — the turning of speech away from its proper course toward another — i.e., from truth to falsehood. They characterize the creation of the angels as what they consider procreation and express it as such; thus, they are fabricators and liars.

His saying, the Exalted: أَصْطَفَى الْبَناتُ عَلَى الْبَنِينَ ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ — "Has He preferred daughters to sons? What is the matter with you? How do you judge? Will you not then take admonition?" (37:153-155).

He repeated the denunciation of the "choosing of daughters" from among the implications of their claim due to its extreme loathsomeness.

Then He rebuked them with His saying: ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ — "What is the matter with you? How do you judge?" because their claim is a judgment without evidence. He followed it with His saying: أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ — "Will you not then take admonition?" as a rebuke and an indication that their claim—besides being without evidence—is contradicted by evidence itself. Had they reflected, it would have been revealed to them that His sanctuary, the Exalted, is hallowed (تنزهت) above being partitioned so as to give birth, or being in need so as to take a child. He has argued against them with this in various places of His speech.

The shift (الالتفات) from the third person to the second person (address) is to indicate the intensity of wrath (السخط) which necessitates rebuking them directly.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَكُمْ سُلْطانٌ مُبِينٌ فَأْتُوا بِكِتابِكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "Do you have a manifest authority? Then bring your scripture, if you are truthful." (37:156-157). The word "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive. What is meant by "authority" (السلطان) — the proof or demonstration — is a scripture revealed from Allah, the Glorified, informing that the angels are His daughters, as suggested by the context. Since this was not established by intellect (عقل) or sense (حس), it remained to be established by a scripture from Allah revealed through revelation (الوحي). If their claim were true and they were truthful in it, they would be able to produce the scripture.

The attribution of the scripture to them (بِكِتابِكُمْ) is based on the assumption of its existence as a proof for their claim.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلُوا بَيْنَهُ وَبَيْنَ الْجِنَّةِ نَسَباً وَلَقَدْ عَلِمَتِ الْجِنَّةُ إِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ — "And they have set up a kinship between Him and the jinn, while the jinn certainly know that they will indeed be hauled [for judgment]." (37:158). Setting up a kinship between Him and the jinn refers to their claim that the jinn are His offspring. The details of their claims were previously discussed in the commentary on Surah Hud regarding the worship of idols.

His saying: وَلَقَدْ عَلِمَتِ الْجِنَّةُ إِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ — "while the jinn certainly know that they will indeed be hauled [for judgment]" means for the reckoning or for the Fire, as suggested by the absolute use of "hauled" (لَمُحْضَرُونَ). In any case, they know that they are subjects (مربوبون) of Allah, who will reckon them and recompense them for what they have done. Thus, between them and Allah, the Glorified, there is the relationship of Lordship (الربوبية) and servitude (العبودية), not the kinship of procreation. Whoever is such does not deserve worship.

It is strange that some have said the "jinn" (الجنة) here refers to a group of angels called by that name, which would necessitate returning the pronoun in "they" (إِنَّهُمْ) to the disbelievers rather than the jinn. This is something for which there is no evidence in His speech, the Exalted, in addition to its distance from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "Immune is Allah from what they describe, except for the chosen servants of Allah." (37:159-160). The pronoun in "they describe" (يَصِفُونَ)—considering the connection of the verse to what precedes it—refers to the aforementioned disbelievers. The exception (الاستثناء) is disjunctive (منقطع). The meaning is: He is hallowed above their description—or above the attributes the disbelievers describe Him with, such as procreation, kinship, partnership, and the like—but the sincere servants of Allah (عباد الله المخلصين) describe Him with a description that befits Him—or with attributes that are worthy of Him.

It has been said that it is a disjunctive exception from the pronoun in "hauled" (لَمُحْضَرُونَ), and it has been said it is from the subject of "they have set up" (جَعَلُوا), with the intervening sentences being a parenthetical insertion. Both of these views are far-fetched.

However, the two verses, when taken independently, possess a broader and more subtle meaning: that the pronoun in "they describe" (يَصِفُونَ) refers to people in general, and the "description" (الوصف) is absolute, encompassing everything any describer attributes to Him. The exception is then continuous (متصل), and the meaning is: He is hallowed above everything that describers attribute to Him, except for the sincere servants of Allah.

This is because people only describe Him using limited concepts (مفاهيم محدودة) they possess, while He, the Glorified, is unlimited; no boundary encompasses Him, and no attribute perceives Him. Everything He is described with, He is more majestic than it; and everything imagined to be Him, He is other than it. However, the Glorified has servants whom He has purified for Himself and reserved for Himself, such that no one else shares in them. He has made Himself known to them and made them forget everything else; they know Him, and they know others through Him. Thus, when they describe Him within their souls, they describe Him with what befits the sanctuary of His Greatness (كبريائه); and when they describe Him with their tongues—while words are deficient and meanings are limited—they admit the inadequacy of expression and acknowledge the fatigue of the tongue.

As the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said—and he is the master of the sincere: لا أحصي ثناء عليك أنت كما أثنيت على نفسك — "I cannot enumerate Your praise; You are as You have praised Yourself." Understand this.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ بِفاتِنِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ هُوَ صالِ الْجَحِيمِ — "For indeed you and what you worship, you cannot beguile [anyone] against Him, except him who is to roast in hell." (37:161-163). This is a derivation (تفريع) from the ruling of the excepted and the excepted-from, or the excepted specifically. The meaning is: since what you have described is misguidance—and the sincere servants of Allah are not misguided in their description—you are not misguiding anyone by it except those who tread the path of the Fire.

It is apparent from the context that "what" (ما) in "what you worship" (ما تَعْبُدُونَ) is a relative pronoun (موصولة) referring to the idols alone, or the idols and the deities of misguidance like the devils of the jinn. The "not" (ما) in "you are not" (ما أَنْتُمْ) is negating (نافية). The pronoun in "against Him" (عَلَيْهِ) refers to Allah, the Glorified, and the prepositional phrase is connected to "beguiling" (بفاتنين). "Beguiling" (فاتنين) is the active participle of "fitnah" (الفتنة) in the sense of misguiding (الإضلال). "Roasting" (صالِ) is from "salw" (الصلو) in the sense of following (الاتباع); thus, the "roaster of hell" (صالي الجحيم) is the follower of hell who treads the path of the Fire. The exception is elliptical (مفرغ), its estimation being: "you cannot beguile anyone except him who is to roast in hell."

The meaning is: indeed you and the deities of misguidance that you worship, all of you together cannot misguide anyone against Allah except him who is a follower of hell.

It has been said that the first "what" (ما) is infinitive (مصدرية) or relative, and the sentence "For indeed you and what you worship" is a complete, independent statement of the type "you and your business" (أنت وشأنك), meaning: "you and what you worship are paired." Then a new sentence begins. It has also been said that "you cannot beguile" (ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ بِفاتِنِينَ) carries the meaning of "compelling" (الحمل), and the pronoun in "against it" (عَلَيْهِ) refers to "what you worship" (ما تَعْبُدُونَ) if "what" is infinitive, or to "what" with an estimated mudaf if it is relative. The meaning would be: "you cannot compel [anyone] to your worship or to the worship of what you worship except him who is to roast in hell."

It was also said that "against" (على) could mean "by" (الباء), and the pronoun refers to "what you worship" or to "what" if it were relative, with "beguiling" (بفاتنين) remaining in its literal sense without implied meaning. The meaning would be: "you cannot misguide anyone by your worship or by the worship of what you worship except..."

All of these are forced interpretations (تكلفات) without necessity. The discussion regarding the shift (الالتفات) in this verse is the same as the discussion regarding it previously.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ — "There is none of us but has a known station. Indeed we are those who stand in ranks. Indeed we are those who celebrate [His] glory." (37:164-166). These three verses—as indicated by the context—are a parenthetical interjection (اعتراض) from the speech of Gabriel or him and his assistants from the angels of revelation (ملائكة الوحي), similar to His saying, the Exalted, in Surah Maryam: وَما نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ لَهُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِينا وَما خَلْفَنا وَما بَيْنَ ذلِكَ — "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between..." (19:64).

It has been said that they are from the speech of the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) describing himself and the believers to the disbelievers as a rebuke and reproach to them, and it is connected to His saying: "Ask them for a pronouncement" (فَاسْتَفْتِهِمْ). The estimation would be: "Ask them and say: there is none of us, the community of Muslims, but has a known station according to his deeds on the Day of Resurrection; and indeed we are those who stand in ranks in prayer, and indeed we are those who celebrate [His] glory." This is a forced interpretation that does not suit the context.

The three verses are intended to refute their claim of the divinity of the angels by citing the angels' own admission of that which negates the disbelievers' claim. They do not deny servitude (العبودية) for the angels; rather, they believe that they are subjects (مربوبون) of Allah, yet lords (أرباب) and deities (آلهة) for those below them, acting independently in what has been delegated to them of the world's affairs without any of this management being linked to Allah, the Glorified. This is what the angels negate for themselves—not their being intermediary causes (أسباب متوسطة) between Him, the Exalted, and His creation, as He, the Exalted, said: بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — "Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command." (21:26-27).

Thus, His saying: وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ — "There is none of us but has a known station" means a designated, specific station in which he has been placed, which he cannot exceed by having an affair delegated to him so that he acts independently in it. Rather, he is naturally disposed (مجبول) toward obedience to Allah in what He commands and toward His worship.

His saying: وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ — "Indeed we are those who stand in ranks" means we stand in ranks before Allah awaiting His commands in the management of the world to carry them out as He wills. As He, the Exalted, said: لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ — "they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they carry out what they are commanded" (66:6). This is what the context suggests. It is sometimes said that the meaning is "we stand in ranks for prayer before Allah," but this is far from the understanding and has no evidence.

His saying: وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ — "Indeed we are those who celebrate [His] glory" means those who hallow Him, the Exalted, from what does not befit the sanctuary of His Greatness. As He, the Exalted, said: يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ — "They celebrate [His] glory night and day, and they do not flag." (21:20).

Thus, the three verses describe the position of the angels in creation and their work appropriate to their creation, which is standing in ranks to receive His command, the Exalted, and hallowing the sanctuary of His Greatness from any partner and everything that does not befit the perfection of His transcendent Essence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كانُوا لَيَقُولُونَ لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدَنا ذِكْراً مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ لَكُنَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "Indeed they used to say, 'Had we a reminder from the ancients, we would have surely been Allah’s chosen servants.'" (37:167-169). This is a return to the previous context.

The pronoun in His saying: "Indeed they used to say" (وَإِنْ كانُوا لَيَقُولُونَ) refers to the Quraysh and those who follow them. The "if" (إِنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy (مخففة من الثقيلة). What is meant by "a reminder from the ancients" (ذِكْراً مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ) is a heavenly scripture of the same kind as the scriptures revealed to the ancients.

The meaning is: "Had we a heavenly scripture of the kind revealed before us to the ancients, we would have been guided and would have been the sincere servants of Allah." They mean that they are excused if they disbelieve because the proof (الحجة) has not been established against them by Allah, the Glorified.

This is, in reality, a lapse (هفوة) on their part, for the creed of idolatry (الوثنية) deems prophethood (النبوة), messengership (الرسالة), and the descent of a heavenly scripture to be impossible.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَفَرُوا بِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ — "But they disbelieved in it; so soon they will know." (37:170). The "so" (الفاء) is elided (فصيحة). The meaning is: "So We revealed to them the Reminder (الذكر), which is the Noble Quran, but they disbelieved in it and did not fulfill what they said; so soon they will know the evil consequence of their disbelief."

This is a threat from Him, the Exalted, to them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has been passed for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped" (37:171-172). His "decree" (كلمته) for them is His word that He spoke regarding them, which is His judgment and ordinance (قضاؤه) concerning them. The "precedence" (سبق) of the decree means its priority in time or its priority in effectiveness and dominance. The preposition "for" (اللام) conveys the meaning of benefit; i.e., "We have decreed a definitive judgment regarding them: that they are the ones who are helped." The speech has been emphasized with various forms of emphasis.

The "help" or "victory" (النصر) has been left absolute without being restricted to this world or the hereafter, or in any other way. Rather, the evidence is to the contrary; He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up" (40:51).

Thus, the messengers (peace be upon them) are helped in argument because they are upon the truth, and the truth is never defeated.

They are helped against their enemies, either by making them prevail over them or by taking vengeance upon them. He, the Exalted, said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى — until He said — حَتَّى إِذَا اسْتَيْأَسَ الرُّسُلُ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا جاءَهُمْ نَصْرُنا فَنُجِّيَ مَنْ نَشاءُ وَلا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُنا عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ — "We did not send [any] before you except men from the people of the towns, to whom We revealed... until when the apostles lost hope and thought they had been called liars, Our help came to them; then We would deliver whomever We wished, and Our punishment is not averted from the guilty lot." (12:109-110).

They are helped in the hereafter, as He, the Exalted, said: يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ — "the day Allah will not let the Prophet down and those who have faith with him" (66:8). A verse in Surah al-Mu'min (Ghafir) was just mentioned with this meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ — "and that Our host will indeed be the victors." (37:173). A "host" (الجند) is a dense gathering, which is why it is used for an army; thus, it is close in meaning to "party" (الحزب). He, the Exalted, said in another place of His speech: وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ اللهِ هُمُ الْغالِبُونَ — "Whoever takes for his guardians Allah, His Apostle and the faithful [should know that] the confederates of Allah are indeed the victors." (5:56).

What is meant by "Our host" (جُنْدَنا) is the community that obeys His command and strives in His way, and they are the believers specifically, or the prophets and those who follow them from the believers. In the second interpretation, there is a generalization after specialization. In any case, the believers are helped just like those they follow among the prophets. He, the Exalted, said: وَلا تَهِنُوا وَلا تَحْزَنُوا وَأَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Do not overtax yourselves and do not grieve, and you shall be the upper ones, should you be faithful." (3:139). Some verses indicating this were just mentioned.

The ruling—namely help and victory—is a social ruling (حكم اجتماعي) contingent upon the title and nothing else. That is, the messengers, who are servants Allah has sent, and the believers, who are a host for Allah acting by His command and striving in His way, as long as they remain upon this description, are helped and victorious. However, if nothing remains of faith but its name, and nothing of the affiliation but a story, then neither help nor victory should be expected.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ — "So leave them alone for a while" (37:174). This is a derivation (تفريع) from the discourse on help and victory. In it is a promise to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of help and victory, and a threat to the polytheists and to the Quraysh specifically.

The command to turn away from them, then making it limited by His saying: "for a while" (حَتَّى حِينٍ), hints that the term is not far off. And so it was; the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) emigrated shortly thereafter, and Allah destroyed the leaders of the Quraysh in the Battle of Badr and others.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَبْصِرْهُمْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ — "and watch them, for soon they will see." (37:175). The command to watch and the informing of their seeing soon, and the conjunction of the speech with the command to turn away immediately, implies—according to the comparison—that the meaning is: "Observe them and see the denial and stubbornness they are upon in the face of your warning and frightening, for soon they will see the evil consequence of their denial and arrogance."

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَبِعَذابِنا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ فَإِذا نَزَلَ بِساحَتِهِمْ فَساءَ صَباحُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ — "Do they seek to hasten Our punishment? But when it descends in their courtyard, evil will be the morning of those who were warned." (37:176-177). This is a rebuke to them for their seeking to hasten and their saying: "When is this promise? When is this victory?" It is an announcement that this punishment is not something that should be hastened, for it results in a miserable day and an ill-omened morning.

The "descending of the punishment in their courtyard" (بِساحَتِهِمْ) is a metaphor for its descending upon them in a manner of encompassment and surrounding. His saying: فَساءَ صَباحُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ — "evil will be the morning of those who were warned" means: "how wretched is their morning as a morning." The "warned" (المنذرون) are the polytheists of the Quraysh.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ وَأَبْصِرْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ — "And leave them alone for a while, and watch, for soon they will see." (37:178-179). This is an emphasis of what has passed by repeating the two verses, as has been said. Some have suggested that what preceded was a threat of the punishment of this world, and this is a threat of the punishment of the hereafter. This is not without merit, for in this verse "and watch" (وَأَبْصِرْ) occurs without an object, unlike the previous verse "and watch them" (وَأَبْصِرْهُمْ). The omission suggests generality—that the meaning is to watch what the generality of people are upon of disbelief and transgression—and this is suited to the threat of the punishment of the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ الْعِزَّةِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ — "Immune is your Lord, the Lord of Might, from what they describe." (37:180). This is a hallowing of Him, the Exalted, from what the disbelievers—who oppose the call of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—describe Him with, as previously mentioned in the Surah.

The proof for this is the attribution of the hallowing to His saying: "your Lord" (رَبِّكَ)—i.e., the Lord whom you worship and to whom you call—and the attribution of "Lord" a second time to "Might" (العزة), which implies His exclusive possession, the Exalted, of Might. Thus, His side is absolutely impregnable; no humiliator can humiliate Him, no victor can defeat Him, and no fleer can escape Him. Therefore, the polytheists, the enemies of the truth who are threatened with punishment, are not able to frustrate Him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَلامٌ عَلَى الْمُرْسَلِينَ — "And peace be to the apostles!" (37:181). This is a salutation upon all the messengers and a protection for them from anything reaching them from Him, the Exalted, that would grieve them or that they would dislike.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "And all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds." (37:182). The discussion regarding this has already passed in the commentary on Surah al-Fatihah.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Muhammad ibn Nadr and Ibn 'Asakir narrated from al-'Ala' ibn Sa'id: that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said one day to those sitting with him: أطت السماء وحق لها أن تئط ، ليس منها موضع قدم إلا عليه ملك راكع أو ساجد — "The heaven has groaned, and it has the right to groan; there is not a foot's space in it except that there is an angel bowing or prostrating upon it." Then he recited: وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ — "Indeed we are those who stand in ranks. Indeed we are those who celebrate [His] glory."

I say: This meaning has been narrated from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through other chains as well.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), when he stood for prayer, would say: استووا تقدم يا فلان تأخر يا فلان أقيموا صفوفكم ـ يريد الله بكم هدى الملائكة — "Stand straight; move forward, O so-and-so; move back, O so-and-so; straighten your ranks—Allah intends for you the guidance of the angels." Then he would recite: وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ.

In Nahj al-Balaghah, he (peace be upon him) said in describing the angels: وصافون لا يتزايلون ومسبحون لا يسأمون — "And they are those who stand in ranks and do not depart, and those who celebrate [His] glory and do not weary."
